Notes: This anniversary marks the release of one of MTV's most successful Unplugged albums, featuring Natalie Merchant's final performance with the band before her departure. The album peaked at number 13 on the Billboard 200 and included memorable acoustic versions of "These Are Days" and "Candy Everybody Wants." The recording captured the band at their creative peak, showcasing their folk-rock sound in an intimate setting that perfectly suited their melodic sensibilities.

Event: As Nasty As They Wanna Be album released
Notes: Today marks the release of one of hip-hop's most controversial albums, which sparked a landmark First Amendment case that went all the way to the Supreme Court. The album was initially banned in several states for its explicit lyrics, leading to the famous Skywalker Records, Inc. v. Navarro case in 1990. Despite the legal challenges, it became a commercial success and helped establish important precedents for artistic expression in music.

Today celebrates the birth of the band's original lead guitarist and primary songwriter, born Matthew Montgomery Roberts. He co-wrote many of the band's biggest hits including "Kryptonite" and "Loser," helping establish their signature post-grunge sound. Roberts was instrumental in the band's rise from regional Mississippi rockers to multi-platinum artists before departing in 2012.

This anniversary celebrates the release of the Mississippi band's breakthrough debut album, which transformed them from local club performers to rock superstars. The album peaked at number 7 on the Billboard 200 and was driven by the massive success of "Kryptonite," which had already gained radio traction years earlier. Featuring hits like "Loser" and "Duck and Run," the album showcased their accessible post-grunge sound and eventually achieved six-times platinum status.

Today marks the release of the band's third studio album, which debuted at number 1 on the Billboard 200 and spawned the hit singles "Let Me Go" and "Behind Those Eyes." The album continued their evolution toward a more polished rock sound while maintaining the melodic hooks that made them famous. Despite mixed critical reception, it achieved platinum certification and demonstrated their continued commercial appeal in the mid-2000s rock landscape.

This date celebrates the birth of the band's original bassist and founding member, who helped establish their signature low-end groove on hits like "Kryptonite" and "When I'm Gone." Harrell was part of the band's core lineup for over two decades before legal troubles led to his departure in 2013. His bass work provided the rhythmic foundation for many of the band's most recognizable songs during their commercial peak.

Today celebrates the birth of the Canadian drummer who joined the band as a touring member and later became a full member. Born Daniel Patrick Adair, he's also known for his work with Nickelback and has brought his powerful, precise drumming style to both bands. His versatility and technical skills have made him one of the most sought-after drummers in modern rock.

This anniversary celebrates the birth of the band's guitarist who joined during their later period, contributing to albums like "Time of My Life" and "Us and the Night." Liles brought his own guitar style to complement Chris Henderson's work, helping expand the band's sound during their evolution beyond their original lineup. His contributions helped maintain the band's guitar-driven identity through various personnel changes.

Event: Us and the Night album released
Notes: Today marks the release of the band's sixth studio album, which represented a return to their heavier rock roots after exploring different sounds on previous releases. The album featured the singles "In the Dark" and "Still Alive" and showcased a more mature songwriting approach while maintaining their radio-friendly appeal. It marked their first album release in five years and demonstrated their continued relevance in the evolving rock landscape.

This date celebrates the birth of the band's lead guitarist and founding member, born Christopher Lee Henderson. He's been the band's primary guitar architect since their formation, crafting the memorable riffs for hits like "Kryptonite" and "When I'm Gone." Henderson's guitar work has remained a constant throughout the band's evolution, providing their signature sound across multiple decades and lineup changes.

Today marks the release of the band's self-titled fourth studio album, which debuted at number 8 on the Billboard 200 and featured the hit singles "It's Not My Time" and "Train." The album showcased a more introspective lyrical approach while maintaining their trademark melodic rock sound. Produced by Howard Benson, it demonstrated their ability to adapt their sound for the late 2000s rock market while staying true to their Mississippi roots.

This anniversary celebrates the release of a stripped-down EP that showcased the band's versatility beyond their electric rock sound. The acoustic format allowed their songwriting and melodic sensibilities to shine through in a more intimate setting. This release demonstrated their ability to reimagine their material and connect with fans in a different musical context.

Event: Time of My Life album released
Notes: Today marks the release of the band's fifth studio album, which peaked at number 3 on the Billboard 200 and featured the singles "When You're Young" and "Every Time You Go." The album represented a sonic evolution with more polished production while addressing themes of life's fleeting moments and personal relationships. It proved their continued commercial viability more than a decade into their career.

This date celebrates the birth of the band's frontman, lead vocalist, and primary drummer, born Bradley Kirk Arnold. He's the creative force behind many of their biggest hits and remains the band's most recognizable member and consistent creative voice. Arnold's distinctive vocals and songwriting have been central to their identity from their early days in Escatawpa, Mississippi, to their multi-platinum success.

Today marks the release of this EP that served as a bridge between "Away from the Sun" and their next full-length album. The release included both new material and alternate versions of existing songs, giving fans additional content during the gap between major releases. It demonstrated the band's commitment to staying connected with their audience between album cycles.

Event: Away from the Sun album released
Notes: This anniversary celebrates the release of the band's sophomore album, which debuted at number 8 on the Billboard 200 and spawned the massive hit "When I'm Gone." The album successfully built upon their debut's success while showing musical growth and more personal songwriting. Featuring additional hits like "Here Without You," it achieved multi-platinum status and proved they weren't a one-album wonder.

Today marks the release of the band's first official greatest hits compilation, celebrating over a decade of commercial success. The collection featured their biggest radio hits including "Kryptonite," "When I'm Gone," and "Here Without You," along with two new tracks. It served as both a retrospective for longtime fans and an introduction for newer listeners to their catalog of hits.

Event: A Six Pack of Hits EP released
Notes: This anniversary marks the release of a collection featuring six of the band's most popular tracks in a convenient package format. The EP served as both a sampler for new fans and a budget-friendly option for existing supporters wanting their biggest hits. It represented the band's continued presence in the marketplace during the transition between full album releases.

Today celebrates the birth of the drummer who joined the band in 2005, replacing Daniel Adair behind the kit. Upchurch brought his own style to the drum throne and contributed to several of the band's later albums including "3 Doors Down" and "Time of My Life." His powerful drumming helped maintain the band's rhythmic foundation during their continued evolution.

Event: Where My Christmas Lives EP released
Notes: This date marks the release of the band's holiday-themed EP, showcasing their take on Christmas music alongside original seasonal material. The release demonstrated their versatility beyond their typical post-grunge sound and gave fans special content for the holiday season. It represented an interesting departure from their usual rock material while maintaining their melodic sensibilities.

Born Jared Joseph Leto, this multi-talented artist celebrates another year as both a Grammy-winning musician and Academy Award-winning actor. He founded 30 Seconds to Mars in 1998 with his brother Shannon, leading the band to platinum success with albums like 'A Beautiful Lie' and 'This Is War'. His dual career in music and film, including his Oscar win for 'Dallas Buyers Club,' makes him one of entertainment's most versatile performers.

Born Donald Newton Van Zant, this Southern rock vocalist celebrates another year as the younger brother of Lynyrd Skynyrd's Ronnie Van Zant and the driving force behind 38 Special. He co-founded the band in 1974 and helped deliver arena rock anthems like 'Hold On Loosely' and 'Caught Up in You' throughout the 1980s. His powerful vocals and stage presence helped establish 38 Special as one of the South's most enduring rock acts.

This guitarist and vocalist celebrates another year as one of 38 Special's primary songwriters and creative forces. Barnes co-wrote many of the band's biggest hits including 'Hold On Loosely' and 'Caught Up in You,' helping define their signature arena rock sound. His guitar work and vocal contributions were instrumental in the band's commercial peak during the early 1980s.

This guitarist celebrates another year as one of the founding members of the Australian pop-rock phenomenon that took the world by storm in the mid-2010s. Clifford's colorful hair and energetic stage presence became signature elements of 5SOS's appeal to their massive teen fanbase. The band achieved remarkable success with multiple chart-topping albums and sold-out stadium tours globally.

Born Curtis James Jackson III, this rapper celebrates another year since rising from Queens street corners to become one of hip-hop's most successful entrepreneurs. His debut album 'Get Rich or Die Tryin'' became a cultural phenomenon in 2003, spawning hits like 'In Da Club' and 'P.I.M.P.' His business ventures and media empire have made him as famous for his entrepreneurial success as his music.

This lead vocalist celebrates another year as the frontman of the Australian quartet that became a global teen sensation. Hemmings' vocals anchored hits like 'She Looks So Perfect' and 'Amnesia,' helping 5 Seconds of Summer achieve multiple platinum albums and worldwide arena tours. The band's blend of pop-punk and mainstream appeal made them one of the most successful acts to emerge from the YouTube generation.

This commercial mixtape compilation marks a controversial moment in hip-hop, debuting at number four on the Billboard 200 despite ongoing legal troubles surrounding the artist. The project featured the viral hit 'FEFE' with Nicki Minaj and showcased the rainbow-haired rapper's aggressive style and internet-savvy promotion tactics. The release demonstrated how social media could drive music success even amid significant controversy.

Hip-hop lost one of its most beloved voices when Malik Isaac Taylor passed away from complications related to diabetes at age 45. As Phife Dawg, he provided the perfect counterbalance to Q-Tip's smooth delivery with his gruff, street-smart rhymes on classics like 'Can I Kick It?' and 'Scenario.' His death came just months before the group's acclaimed reunion album 'We Got It From Here,' which served as both a comeback and a tribute to his legacy.

Born Kamaal Ibn John Fareed, this visionary rapper and producer celebrates another year as one of hip-hop's most influential artists. As the primary creative force behind A Tribe Called Quest, he helped pioneer the jazz-influenced alternative hip-hop sound that defined the Native Tongues movement. His smooth delivery and innovative production work on classics like 'Can I Kick It?' and 'Scenario' established him as both a masterful MC and beatmaker.

Event: Peope's Instinctive Travels and the Paths of Rhythm
Notes: This groundbreaking debut album marks the arrival of one of hip-hop's most innovative groups, introducing their unique blend of jazz samples and conscious rap to the world. The album peaked at number 91 on the Billboard 200 but became a critical favorite, featuring the iconic single 'Can I Kick It?' with its Lou Reed sample. The record established the template for alternative hip-hop and helped launch the influential Native Tongues collective.

Born Jarobi White, this founding member celebrates another year as part of A Tribe Called Quest's original lineup, though he left the group before their commercial peak to pursue culinary interests. He appeared on the group's classic debut 'People's Instinctive Travels and the Paths of Rhythm' before stepping away from music. White later rejoined the group for their 2016 reunion album, bringing the original quartet full circle.

Event: Beats, Rhymes and Life album released
Notes: This fourth studio album marks a commercial peak for the group, debuting at number eight on the Billboard 200 and earning platinum certification. The album featured the hit single 'Stressed Out' and showcased a more mainstream sound that divided some longtime fans but expanded their audience significantly. Despite the commercial success, it would be their final album for 18 years before their surprise 2016 reunion.

This DJ and producer celebrates another year as the sonic architect behind some of hip-hop's most innovative beats. As A Tribe Called Quest's primary beatmaker, Muhammad crafted the jazz-influenced production that defined albums like 'The Low End Theory' and 'Midnight Marauders.' His work with the Ummah production collective also shaped classic albums for artists like D'Angelo and Busta Rhymes.

Event: The Low End Theory album released
Notes: This sophomore album marks the creative and commercial peak of alternative hip-hop, reaching number 45 on the Billboard 200 and earning widespread critical acclaim. The record perfected the group's jazz-influenced sound with classics like 'Scenario' featuring Busta Rhymes and 'Check the Rhime,' built around Ron Carter's bass lines. Rolling Stone later ranked it among the greatest albums of all time, cementing its status as a hip-hop masterpiece.

This fifth studio album marks the end of an era, serving as the group's final release for 18 years following internal tensions and changing musical landscapes. The album reached number 25 on the Billboard 200 and featured the single 'Find a Way,' showcasing their continued evolution while maintaining their signature sound. Though less commercially successful than previous efforts, it concluded their initial run on a creatively satisfying note.

This third studio album represents the group's commercial breakthrough, reaching number eight on the Billboard 200 and achieving platinum status. The record featured hip-hop classics like 'Award Tour' and 'Electric Relaxation,' perfectly balancing accessibility with their signature jazz-influenced innovation. The album's success established them as one of hip-hop's premier acts and influenced countless alternative rap artists who followed.

Event: We Got It From Here... Thank You 4 Your Service album released
Notes: This surprise reunion album marks both a triumphant return and a bittersweet farewell, released eight months after Phife Dawg's death and 18 years after their previous studio effort. The album debuted at number one on the Billboard 200, featuring collaborations with Kendrick Lamar, Anderson .Paak, and others while addressing social issues and the group's legacy. It serves as both a fitting tribute to Phife and proof that the group's innovative spirit remained intact.

Born Malik Isaac Taylor, this legendary MC celebrates another year as hip-hop's most beloved underdog, whose gruff delivery perfectly complemented Q-Tip's smooth flow. Known as the 'Five-Foot Assassin,' he delivered memorable verses on classics like 'Can I Kick It?' and stole the show on 'Scenario' with his energetic performance. His street-smart lyricism and infectious personality made him a fan favorite and essential element of the group's chemistry.

Event: Take On Me hits #1 on US singles charts
Notes: They become the first Norwegian group to score a US No. 1

Born Aaliyah Dana Haughton, this R&B princess celebrates another year as one of music's most promising young talents, whose smooth vocals and innovative style helped redefine contemporary soul music. Working with producers like Timbaland and Missy Elliott, she created groundbreaking albums like 'One in a Million' that blended R&B with futuristic production. Her influence on fashion, music videos, and vocal styling made her a trendsetter who inspired countless artists across multiple genres.

Event: Age Ain't Nothing But a Number album released
Notes: Marks the debut of the R&B prodigy who was just 15 years old when this album hit shelves. The record reached number 18 on the Billboard 200 and spawned hits like the title track and "Back & Forth," establishing her as a major new voice in contemporary R&B. Produced by R. Kelly, the album showcased her smooth vocals and mature sound that would influence a generation of artists.

Celebrates the birth of the blonde half of ABBA's iconic female duo. Born Agnetha Åse Fältskog, she was already a successful solo artist in Sweden before ABBA's formation and provided lead vocals on classics like "SOS" and "The Winner Takes It All." Her distinctive voice and stage presence helped make ABBA one of the best-selling pop groups of all time.

Honors the birth of AC/DC's rhythm guitar powerhouse and the band's musical architect. Malcolm Mitchell Young co-founded the band with his younger brother Angus in 1973 and was the driving force behind their signature hard rock sound. His solid rhythm guitar work and songwriting formed the backbone of classics like "Highway to Hell" and "Back in Black."

Event: Blow Up Your Video album released
Notes: Commemorates the Australian rockers' eleventh studio album, which marked their return after a three-year hiatus. The record peaked at number 12 on the Billboard 200 and featured the singles "Heatseeker" and "That's the Way I Wanna Rock 'n' Roll." While not matching their previous commercial peaks, it proved the band could still deliver their trademark high-energy rock after the massive success of their 1980s albums.

Celebrates AC/DC's debut album release in their native Australia, featuring a young Bon Scott on vocals. This original version included tracks like "Baby, Please Don't Go" and "She's Got Balls" that showcased the band's raw, blues-influenced hard rock sound. The album established their reputation in Australia before they conquered the international market with later releases.

Marks a tragic turning point for AC/DC when their charismatic frontman Ronald Belford Scott passed away in London at age 33. Scott had defined the band's wild image and contributed his distinctive vocals to classics like "Highway to Hell" and "T.N.T." His death initially threatened the band's future, but they honored his memory by recruiting Brian Johnson and creating their biggest album, "Back in Black."

Commemorates the band's fourteenth studio album, which proved they could still rock hard in the new millennium. The record debuted at number seven on the Billboard 200 and featured the title track as its lead single, showcasing their unchanged commitment to straight-ahead rock and roll. It was their first studio album in eight years and demonstrated that their classic sound remained timeless.

Celebrates the birth of the bassist who played on some of AC/DC's most crucial early recordings. Mark Whitmore Evans joined the band in 1975 and played on "T.N.T.," "High Voltage," and "Dirty Deeds Done Dirt Cheap," helping establish their international sound. Though his tenure was relatively brief, ending in 1977, his contributions were vital during the band's formative years.

Event: Let There Be Rock album released
Notes: Marks the release of AC/DC's fourth studio album, widely considered one of their finest achievements. The record featured classics like the title track and "Whole Lotta Rosie," showcasing Bon Scott's charismatic vocals and the Young brothers' powerful guitar work. It reached number 17 in Australia and helped establish their reputation as one of the world's premier hard rock bands.

Honors the birth of rock's most recognizable schoolboy, born Angus McKinnon Young in Glasgow, Scotland. The lead guitarist's energetic stage presence, complete with his trademark school uniform, became AC/DC's visual signature alongside his blistering guitar solos. His playing on songs like "Thunderstruck" and "Highway to Hell" has made him one of rock's most influential guitarists.
Brian replaced Bon Scott who died after binge drinking in Feb 1980

Commemorates AC/DC's first international album release, adapting tracks from their Australian albums for worldwide audiences. This version featured different track selections than the original Australian release and included "T.N.T." and "High Voltage," introducing global audiences to their hard-driving sound. The album established them in the UK and US markets, setting the stage for their eventual worldwide success.

Celebrates the release of what many consider AC/DC's most underrated album, featuring some of their heaviest material. Recorded during a productive period, it included tracks like "Rock 'n' Roll Damnation" and "Sin City" that showcased their evolving songwriting abilities. While it didn't achieve the commercial success of later albums, it's praised by fans and critics for its raw power and consistency.

Marks the birth of the New Zealand-born drummer who provided the thunderous backbeat for AC/DC's most successful era. Phillip Hugh Norman Rudd joined the band in 1975 and played on classics like "Highway to Hell" and "Back in Black," contributing to their signature tight, driving rhythm section. His powerful, no-frills drumming style became an essential element of their sound.

Commemorates the soundtrack album that accompanied Stephen King's film "Maximum Overdrive," featuring a mix of new tracks and classic AC/DC material. The title track became a hit single, and the album reached number 33 on the Billboard 200, introducing their music to a new generation through the movie tie-in. It served as an effective greatest hits collection while providing new material for longtime fans.

Celebrates the birth of the English drummer who filled the challenging role of replacing Phil Rudd during AC/DC's mid-1980s period. Wright played on albums like "Fly on the Wall" and "Blow Up Your Video," maintaining the band's signature rhythm section sound during a transitional period. His technical skills and rock-solid timing helped keep AC/DC's momentum going through lineup changes.

Event: Celine Dion cover of "You Shook Me..." voted worst cover ever
Notes: Total Guitar magazine readers voted Celine's cover of You Shook Me All Night Long as the worst cover song of all-time

Event: Fly on the Wall album released
Notes: Marks the release of AC/DC's tenth studio album, which found them exploring a slightly more experimental sound while maintaining their hard rock core. The record included tracks like the title song and "Sink the Pink," though it received mixed reviews from critics and fans. Despite not matching their previous commercial peaks, it demonstrated the band's willingness to evolve while staying true to their rock and roll roots.

Celebrates the birth of AC/DC's legendary frontman, born Ronald Belford Scott in Forfar, Scotland. Scott's raspy voice, wild stage antics, and clever lyrics made him one of rock's most charismatic performers, defining the band's image on classics like "Highway to Hell" and "T.N.T." His larger-than-life personality and vocal style helped establish AC/DC as hard rock legends before his untimely death in 1980.

Commemorates one of rock's greatest albums and a tribute to fallen frontman Bon Scott, featuring new vocalist Brian Johnson. The record became one of the best-selling albums of all time, reaching number four on the Billboard 200 and spawning classics like the title track and "You Shook Me All Night Long." Its massive success proved that AC/DC could overcome tragedy and create their most enduring musical statement.

This marks the anniversary of what many consider the band's masterpiece and their final album with Bon Scott. The title track became one of their most enduring anthems, while the album showcased the quintet at their creative and commercial peak. Producer Robert John 'Mutt' Lange helped craft their most polished sound to date, setting the stage for global superstardom.

Event: Flick of the Switch album released
Notes: This release celebrates the band's ninth studio effort, which marked a deliberate return to a rawer, less produced sound after the massive success of Back in Black. The self-produced album featured harder-edged tracks like 'Nervous Shakedown' and showed the group's determination to stay true to their roots. Despite mixed critical reception, it demonstrated their refusal to repeat previous formulas.

Event: Dirty Deeds Done Cheap album released
Notes: This anniversary commemorates the Australian release of their third studio album, though it wouldn't reach American audiences until 1981. The title track became one of their most recognizable songs, while tracks like 'Problem Child' and 'Ride On' showcased Bon Scott's storytelling prowess. The album's delayed international release helped fuel the band's mystique in overseas markets.

This release marked the band's successful return after a five-year hiatus, proving their enduring power in the post-hair metal era. The album spawned the hit 'Thunderstruck,' which became a stadium anthem and one of their most streamed songs decades later. Produced by Bruce Fairbairn, it showed the band adapting to '90s production while maintaining their signature sound.

This anniversary celebrates the band's thirteenth studio album and their first collection of entirely new material in five years. The release coincided with their induction into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame nomination discussions, cementing their legacy status. While not matching their earlier commercial peaks, tracks like 'Hard as a Rock' proved their riff-writing abilities remained sharp.

Event: Mayor of Melbourne officially opened 'AC/DC' Lane
Notes: The Lord Mayor erected the sign to cheers and bagpipes playing the bands song 'Long Way To The Top'

Born Brian Francis Johnson in Dunston, England, he became one of hard rock's most distinctive voices after joining the band in 1980 following Bon Scott's death. His debut album Back in Black became one of the best-selling albums of all time, while his raspy, powerful vocals helped define the band's sound for over three decades. His working-class background and car racing passion made him a natural fit for the group's blue-collar rock aesthetic.

Event: If You Want Blood You've Got It live album released
Notes: This live album captures the band at their peak with Bon Scott, recorded during their 1978 European tour. The raw energy of tracks like 'Whole Lotta Rosie' and 'Problem Child' demonstrated why they were becoming one of the world's premier live acts. The album served as a bridge between their Australian success and international breakthrough, showcasing their concert power to a global audience.

This comeback album marked the band's return after an eight-year recording hiatus, their longest gap between studio releases. Despite being available exclusively through Walmart in the US, it debuted at number one in 29 countries and became their second-best-selling album. The title track and 'Rock N Roll Train' proved the band could still craft memorable anthems in their sixth decade.
A statue honouring AC/DC's Bon Scott was unveiled at the Fremantle Fishing Boat Harbour in Western Australia

Born Christopher Slade in Pontypridd, Wales, he brought his powerful drumming style to the band during a crucial period from 1989 to 1994. His work on The Razor's Edge helped revitalize their sound for the 1990s, while his dynamic stage presence enhanced their live performances. Before joining, he had already established himself with Uriah Heep and later continued with The Firm and other notable rock acts.

Event: Black Ice hits #1 on US album charts
Notes: The band's 15th studio album, Black Ice went to #1 in 29 countries.

This comprehensive box set celebrates the Bon Scott era with rare tracks, alternate versions, and previously unreleased material spanning 1974-1979. The collection includes the complete early albums plus a disc of rarities that gave fans deeper insight into the band's formative years. Released twenty years after Scott's death, it served as both tribute and historical document of their classic lineup.

This marks the passing of the band's rhythm guitarist and primary songwriter, who died from complications related to dementia at age 64. Malcolm was the driving force behind the band's signature sound, crafting the thunderous riffs that powered classics like 'Back in Black' and 'Highway to Hell.' His partnership with brother Angus formed one of rock's most formidable creative teams, with Malcolm preferring to stay in the background while anchoring their musical foundation.

Event: For Those About to Rock album released
Notes: This album marked the band's ambitious follow-up to Back in Black, featuring the cannon-firing title track that became a concert staple. While it reached number one in several countries, it faced the impossible task of matching its predecessor's massive success. The album showcased a slightly more experimental approach, with longer compositions and the famous cannon sound effects that became part of their live show spectacle.

This release became particularly poignant as it featured Malcolm Young's final recorded contributions before his retirement due to dementia. The album's title proved prophetic, as it captured the band at a crossroads between their legendary past and uncertain future. Despite the internal challenges, tracks like 'Play Ball' demonstrated their ability to create driving rock anthems well into their fifth decade.

This early album established many of the band's signature elements, including the explosive title track that became their first major anthem. Originally released only in Australia and New Zealand, it featured Bon Scott's most confident vocal performances to date and showcased their evolving hard rock sound. The album's success in their home market proved they were ready for international expansion.

Born in Glasgow, Scotland, he stepped into one of rock's most challenging roles when he replaced his uncle Malcolm Young on rhythm guitar in 2014. As Malcolm's nephew and Angus's nephew, music runs deep in his family bloodline, having previously played with City Kidd and other bands. His seamless integration into the group helped ensure their musical legacy continued despite the difficult circumstances surrounding Malcolm's illness.

Born Clifford Williams in Romford, England, he joined the band in 1977 and became their longest-serving bass player, anchoring their rhythm section for over four decades. His solid, understated playing style perfectly complemented the Young brothers' guitar attack, while his backing vocals added depth to their sound. Though he announced retirement in 2016, he returned for their 2020 album, proving his enduring commitment to the band.

Born Adele Laurie Blue Adkins in Tottenham, London, she became one of the 21st century's biggest-selling artists with her powerhouse vocals and deeply personal songwriting. Her albums 19, 21, and 25 each topped charts worldwide, with 21 becoming one of the best-selling albums of all time and earning her six Grammy Awards in one night. Her ability to blend contemporary pop with classic soul influences has made her a defining voice of her generation.

Event: Rolling In The Deep goes #1 on US singles charts
Notes: This marks the moment when the British powerhouse's comeback reached its peak, as her soulful breakup anthem topped the Billboard Hot 100. The song spent seven weeks at number one and became her first US chart-topper, transforming her from a promising debut artist into a global superstar. Rolling In The Deep's gospel-tinged vocals and driving beat helped establish the template for her massive success with 21.

Event: Hello goes #1 on US Singles Charts
Notes: This celebrates another chart milestone as her piano-driven comeback single debuted at number one on the Hot 100. Hello marked her triumphant return after a four-year hiatus and became the first song to sell over one million digital copies in a single week. The track's emotional vulnerability and soaring vocals perfectly set the stage for her record-breaking album 25.

Today marks the birthday of Bradford Ernest Whitford, the steady rhythm guitarist whose melodic sensibilities helped define Boston's Bad Boys from Beantown. His partnership with Joe Perry created one of rock's most distinctive dual-guitar attacks, contributing to classics like Sweet Emotion and Walk This Way. Whitford's understated style provided the perfect complement to Perry's flashier lead work throughout the band's golden era.

This anniversary celebrates the release of the band's comeback album that proved their late-career resilience with multi-platinum success. Despite mixed critical reception, Nine Lives spawned hits like Pink and I Don't Want to Miss a Thing, with the latter becoming their first number-one single. The album marked their continued commercial viability in the alternative rock era, selling over two million copies in the US.

Today celebrates the birth of Steven Victor Tallarico, the flamboyant frontman whose operatic wails and manic stage presence made him one of rock's most iconic performers. His collaborations with Joe Perry produced timeless classics like Dream On and Sweet Emotion, while his later reinvention on American Idol introduced him to new generations. Tyler's four-octave range and theatrical showmanship helped establish the template for rock stardom.

Event: Toys in the Attic album released
Notes: This anniversary marks the release of the album that transformed the Boston rockers from promising upstarts into hard rock legends. Toys in the Attic spawned career-defining classics like Sweet Emotion and Walk This Way, establishing their signature blend of blues-based riffs and pop sensibilities. The album's success launched them into the upper echelon of American rock bands and remains their creative peak.

Their 11th studio album which became their best selling album to date with sales over 20m. The album which featured the hits 'Livin' On The Edge' and 'Crazy' also featured guests Don Henley and Lenny Kravitz.

Today marks the birthday of Joseph Michael Kramer, the thunderous drummer whose powerful backbeat anchored some of hard rock's most enduring anthems. His precise timing and explosive fills provided the rhythmic foundation for classics like Walk This Way and Back in the Saddle. Kramer's steady presence behind the kit helped the band weather decades of lineup changes and personal turmoil.

This anniversary celebrates the album that marked their remarkable commercial comeback after years of drug-fueled decline and internal strife. Permanent Vacation spawned MTV favorites like Dude Looks Like a Lady and Angel, proving they could adapt their blues-rock sound for the video age. The album's success launched their second act as arena-filling superstars and multi-platinum hitmakers.

Today celebrates the birth of Anthony Joseph Perry, the guitar virtuoso whose riff-heavy style became synonymous with American hard rock. His partnership with Steven Tyler created some of rock's most enduring songs, from Dream On to Walk This Way, while his Gibson Les Paul became his signature weapon. Perry's blues-influenced leads and memorable hooks helped establish the band as America's answer to the Rolling Stones.

This anniversary marks the release of their commercial peak, an album that solidified their late-career renaissance with multiple hit singles. Pump delivered chart-toppers like Love in an Elevator and Janie's Got a Gun, showcasing their ability to blend hard rock with contemporary production. The album spent five weeks at number five and spawned four top-40 hits, cementing their status as MTV superstars.

They played at Nipmuc Regional High School in Mendon, Massachusetts. Aerosmith became the best-selling American rock band of all time, having sold more than 150 million albums worldwide. They also hold the record for the most gold and multi-platinum albums by an American group.

Today marks the birthday of Thomas William Hamilton, the melodic bassist whose fluid lines provided the rhythmic backbone for the Bad Boys from Boston. His memorable bass work on classics like Sweet Emotion and Walk This Way helped define the band's signature sound throughout their multi-decade career. Hamilton's New Year's Eve birth makes him one of rock's most consistently celebrating musicians.

Born Joseph Edgar Foreman, this celebrates the birthday of the rapper who achieved unexpected mainstream success with his laid-back comedy hip-hop style. His novelty hit Because I Got High became a cultural phenomenon in 2001, reaching number 13 on the Billboard Hot 100 and earning a Grammy nomination. The Mississippi-born artist's humorous take on stoner culture made him a unlikely MTV favorite during the early 2000s.

Today celebrates the birthday of the Richmond, Virginia-born singer-songwriter who transitioned from new wave success with 'Til Tuesday to acclaimed solo artistry. Her intelligent, melancholy pop earned critical praise and an Oscar nomination for Magnolia's Save Me, while albums like Bachelor No. 2 showcased her gift for crafting emotionally complex character studies. Mann's literate songwriting and distinctive voice made her an indie rock favorite throughout the 1990s and beyond.

Today marks the birthday of the Australian vocalist whose soaring tenor helped define the soft rock sound of the late 1970s and early 1980s. His partnership with Graham Russell produced massive adult contemporary hits like All Out of Love and Every Woman in the World, making them one of the era's most successful ballad specialists. Hitchcock's powerful falsetto became the group's signature element across their string of romantic chart-toppers.

Today celebrates the birthday of Ryan Met, the multi-instrumentalist middle brother of the New York indie pop trio known for their quirky, sample-heavy sound. As the band's primary producer and arranger, he helped craft their distinctive blend of vintage samples, modern beats, and introspective lyrics. Met's production skills were instrumental in creating hits like Weak and Bang from their bedroom studio setup.

This anniversary marks the release of the New York brothers' debut album, recorded entirely in their apartment living room using vintage equipment and creative sampling. The DIY effort showcased their unique blend of indie pop, electronic elements, and nostalgic sounds, laying the groundwork for their future success. Living Room's homemade aesthetic and inventive production techniques helped establish their reputation as innovative young artists in the indie scene.

This anniversary celebrates the release of their fourth studio album, which debuted at number 10 on the Billboard 200 and marked their highest chart position to date. OK Orchestra featured the hit single Bang, which became their biggest mainstream success and showcased their evolved sound combining orchestral elements with electronic pop. The album demonstrated their growth from bedroom producers to major-label artists capable of filling arenas.

This anniversary marks the release of their ambitious third album, a conceptual work exploring themes of growing up and losing innocence. Neotheater spawned the hit 100 Bad Days and showcased the brothers' maturing songwriting abilities, blending their signature quirky production with more serious lyrical content. The album debuted at number 8 on the Billboard 200, establishing them as rising stars in the alternative pop landscape.

Marks the release of the breakthrough album that propelled the indie pop trio into mainstream success. The album peaked at number 61 on the Billboard 200 and spawned the massive hit "Weak," which became a viral sensation and reached the top 10 on multiple charts. Recorded in their New York City apartment using DIY production techniques, the album showcased their distinctive blend of electronic pop and introspective lyrics that resonated with a generation of listeners.

Celebrates the birthday of the youngest Met brother and lead vocalist of the indie pop trio AJR. Jack serves as the primary songwriter and producer for the band alongside his older brothers Adam and Ryan. His distinctive vocals and creative vision helped shape hits like "Weak" and "Bang!" that have garnered billions of streams worldwide.

Marks the birthday of the eldest Met brother and bassist of AJR. Adam, who goes by his full name Adam Brett Met professionally, co-founded the band with his younger brothers Jack and Ryan in 2005. He plays a crucial role in the band's songwriting process and has helped guide their evolution from street performers in Central Park to chart-topping artists.

Celebrates the release of AJR's fifth studio album, which debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200. The album features the hit single "Yes I'm A Mess" and showcases the band's continued evolution of their signature orchestral-pop sound. The record explores themes of self-doubt and personal growth with their characteristic blend of electronic production and live instrumentation.

Event: Land of the Midnight Sun album released
Notes: Marks the release of the jazz fusion guitarist's breakthrough solo album following his departure from Return to Forever. The album showcased Di Meola's lightning-fast technique and melodic sensibilities across eight instrumental tracks that blended jazz, rock, and Mediterranean influences. Recorded when he was just 22 years old, it established him as one of the premier guitarists in the fusion movement and featured the standout track "Race with Devil on Spanish Highway."

Celebrates the birthday of Albert Leornes Greene, the legendary soul and gospel singer known as the Reverend Al Green. His smooth falsetto and passionate delivery produced classics like "Let's Stay Together," "Tired of Being Alone," and "Love and Happiness" in the early 1970s. He later became an ordained minister and seamlessly blended his secular and spiritual careers, earning induction into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1995.

Event: Grammy Award Win - Best Alternative Music Album - "Sound & Color"
Notes: Commemorates Alabama Shakes' Grammy victory for their sophomore album "Sound & Color," which marked a creative breakthrough for the Athens band. The win recognized their evolution from garage rock revivalists to sophisticated genre-blenders, incorporating elements of psychedelia, R&B, and experimental rock. Brittany Howard's powerful vocals and the band's adventurous arrangements earned critical acclaim and cemented their status as one of rock's most important contemporary acts.

Event: Grammy Award Win - Best Rock Performance - "Don't Wanna Fight"
Notes: Celebrates Alabama Shakes' Grammy win for "Don't Wanna Fight," the lead single from their album "Sound & Color." The track showcased Brittany Howard's commanding vocal performance over a hypnotic groove that blended classic soul with modern alternative rock. This recognition highlighted the band's ability to create both commercially appealing and artistically ambitious music.

Event: Grammy Award Win - Best Rock Song - "Don't Wanna Fight"
Notes: Marks Alabama Shakes' Grammy recognition for "Don't Wanna Fight" in the songwriting category. The award honored Brittany Howard's compositional skills and the band's collaborative approach to crafting songs that resonated with both critics and audiences. This victory underscored their impact on contemporary rock and soul music.

Celebrates the birthday of Alabama Shakes' bassist, who provided the rhythmic foundation for the band's powerful sound. Cockrell's bass work was integral to hits like "Hold On" and "Don't Wanna Fight," anchoring Brittany Howard's soaring vocals and the band's dynamic arrangements. His contributions helped establish Alabama Shakes as one of the most important rock bands of the 2010s.

Marks the birthday of Alabama Shakes' guitarist, whose versatile playing complemented Brittany Howard's vocals and songwriting. Fogg's guitar work ranged from driving rock riffs to subtle textural elements, contributing to the band's evolution from their raw debut "Boys & Girls" to the more experimental "Sound & Color." His musical partnership with Howard was crucial to the band's Grammy-winning success.

Celebrates the birthday of the powerhouse vocalist and primary songwriter who led Alabama Shakes to international acclaim. Howard's commanding voice and guitar skills drove hits like "Hold On" and "Don't Wanna Fight," earning the band multiple Grammy Awards. She has since launched a successful solo career while remaining one of the most distinctive voices in contemporary rock and soul music.

Marks the birthday of one of country music's most successful and traditional artists. Jackson became a defining voice of 1990s country with hits like "Chattahoochee," "She's Got The Rhythm," and "Gone Country," selling over 75 million records worldwide. His commitment to traditional country sounds and storytelling has earned him induction into both the Country Music Hall of Fame and the Songwriters Hall of Fame.

Celebrates the birthday of the British musician, producer, and audio engineer who co-founded The Alan Parsons Project. Parsons previously worked as an engineer on landmark albums including The Beatles' "Abbey Road" and Pink Floyd's "The Dark Side of the Moon." His progressive rock project with Eric Woolfson produced conceptual albums like "I Robot" and "Eye in the Sky" that showcased sophisticated production techniques and atmospheric soundscapes.

Marks the birthday of the Canadian-American singer-songwriter who became a defining voice of 1990s alternative rock. Her breakthrough album "Jagged Little Pill" featured raw, confessional lyrics and spawned hits like "You Oughta Know" and "Ironic." She has won seven Grammy Awards and sold over 75 million records worldwide while influencing countless female artists with her uncompromising artistic vision.

Commemorates the release of one of the best-selling albums of all time, which spent 12 weeks at number one and sold over 33 million copies worldwide. The album transformed Morissette from a teen pop star into a alternative rock icon with hits like "You Oughta Know," "Hand in My Pocket," and "Ironic." Produced by Glen Ballard, the record's raw emotional honesty and guitar-driven sound became a blueprint for female singer-songwriters in the 1990s.
Blues singer and guitarist best known for Born Under a Bad Sign

Marks the release of Alice Cooper's fourth studio album, which featured the shock rock anthem "Under My Wheels" and helped establish their theatrical hard rock sound. The album reached number 21 on the Billboard 200 and showcased the band's ability to blend heavy guitar riffs with Alice Cooper's macabre stage persona. Producer Bob Ezrin's influence helped refine their sound, setting the stage for their breakthrough with "School's Out" the following year.

Celebrates the birthday of the original Alice Cooper band's bassist and co-founder. Dunaway was instrumental in developing the band's early sound and co-wrote several of their classic songs including "I'm Eighteen" and "School's Out." His bass playing provided the foundation for Alice Cooper's theatrical shock rock performances that revolutionized rock concerts in the early 1970s.

Marks the birthday of Vincent Damon Furnier, the shock rock pioneer who revolutionized theatrical performance in rock music. Cooper's macabre stage shows featuring guillotines, snakes, and elaborate costumes influenced generations of performers while hits like "School's Out" and "No More Mr. Nice Guy" became rock classics. He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2011 and continues to tour with his legendary horror-inspired performances.

Event: Welcome To My Nightmare album released
Notes: Marks the anniversary of Cooper's first solo album following his split from the original Alice Cooper band. The conceptual album reached #5 on the Billboard 200 and spawned the hit single "Only Women Bleed," showcasing Cooper's theatrical vision through a horror-themed narrative. The album marked a successful transition from band leader to solo artist and established the template for his future theatrical rock operas.

Celebrates the release of Cooper's comeback album after several years of commercial decline. The record marked a return to his theatrical horror roots and featured collaborations with notable musicians including Chris Cornell and Rob Zombie. While not a major commercial success, it signaled Cooper's artistic resurgence and helped revitalize his career for the 1990s.

Marks the anniversary of the grunge legends' acoustic-driven EP that became their most commercially successful release. The six-track collection debuted at #1 on the Billboard 200, making it the first EP ever to achieve this feat. Featuring haunting tracks like "No Excuses" and "I Stay Away," it showcased the band's versatility beyond their heavy metal sound during the height of their popularity.

Celebrates the release of the band's second EP, featuring four tracks including the Chris Cornell collaboration "Right Turn." The acoustic-leaning collection served as a bridge between their breakthrough album Facelift and their landmark release Dirt. The EP demonstrated the band's experimental side and included guest vocals from Ann Wilson of Heart on "Brother" and "Am I Inside."

Celebrates the birthday of the band's guitarist, songwriter, and creative force whose distinctive guitar work and vocal harmonies defined Alice In Chains' sound. Born Jerry Fulton Cantrell Jr., he wrote most of the band's music and lyrics, creating the dark, heavy compositions that became grunge classics. His guitar playing combines metal riffing with melodic sensibilities, and he successfully led the band's continuation after Layne Staley's death.

Marks the birthday of the band's bassist who joined Alice In Chains in 1993, replacing original member Mike Starr. Born Michael Allen Inez, he previously played with Ozzy Osbourne and brought a solid foundation to the band's rhythm section. His bass work can be heard on classic albums like Jar of Flies and the self-titled Alice In Chains, and he remains an integral part of the band's current lineup.

Celebrates the birthday of Alice In Chains' drummer and one of the band's founding members alongside Layne Staley and Jerry Cantrell. Born Sean Howard Kinney, his powerful, precise drumming provided the backbone for the band's heavy sound throughout their career. He's the only member to appear on every Alice In Chains release and has been instrumental in keeping the band together through various lineup changes and tragedies.

Marks the anniversary of the band's major label debut that helped launch the grunge movement. The album reached #42 on the Billboard 200 and was eventually certified double-platinum, featuring breakthrough singles "Would?" and "Man in the Box." The heavy, dark sound combined metal with alternative rock in a way that distinguished Alice In Chains from their Seattle contemporaries and established them as grunge pioneers.

Celebrates the birthday of Alice In Chains' original lead vocalist whose haunting voice and introspective lyrics became synonymous with the grunge era. Born Layne Thomas Staley, his powerful vocal range and harmonies with Jerry Cantrell created some of rock's most memorable moments on classics like "Man in the Box" and "Would?." His struggles with addiction became a central theme in the band's music, and his death in 2002 marked the end of an era for grunge music.

Marks the anniversary of the band's sixth studio album, their third with vocalist William DuVall. The album debuted at #12 on the Billboard 200 and was praised for recapturing the dark, heavy sound of their 1990s classics. Featuring singles like "The One You Know" and "So Far Under," it demonstrated that Alice In Chains could still create compelling music nearly three decades after their formation.

Celebrates the birthday of the vocalist and guitarist who joined Alice In Chains in 2006 following Layne Staley's death. Born William Bradley DuVall, he previously fronted the band Comes with the Fall and brought his own vocal style while respecting the band's legacy. His addition allowed Alice In Chains to continue touring and recording, contributing to albums like Black Gives Way to Blue and Rainier Fog while maintaining the band's distinctive sound.

Marks the anniversary of Alice In Chains' masterpiece and one of grunge's defining albums. The record peaked at #6 on the Billboard 200 and was eventually certified quadruple-platinum, featuring classics like "Them Bones," "Rooster," and "Down in a Hole." The album's themes of addiction, war, and personal struggle, combined with its incredibly heavy yet melodic sound, cemented the band's place as one of the most important acts of the 1990s.

Event: Black Gives Way To Blue album released
Notes: Celebrates the band's emotional comeback album, their first studio release in 14 years and first with new vocalist William DuVall. The album debuted at #5 on the Billboard 200 and featured the Grammy-nominated title track, a tribute to late vocalist Layne Staley with guest vocals from Elton John. The record proved Alice In Chains could honor their past while moving forward, marking a successful new chapter in their career.

Marks the anniversary of the band's self-titled third album, often called "Tripod" due to its three-legged dog cover art. The album debuted at #1 on the Billboard 200 despite minimal promotion, featuring darker, more experimental tracks like "Grind" and "Heaven Beside You." Recorded during Layne Staley's deepest struggles with addiction, it showcased a more introspective and haunting side of the band's sound.

Celebrates the birthday of the R&B superstar born Alicia Augello Cook who revolutionized contemporary soul music. Her 2001 debut album Songs in A Minor won five Grammy Awards and established her as a major force in music, blending classical piano training with hip-hop influenced R&B. She's sold over 65 million records worldwide and is known for hits like "Fallin'" and "No One," plus her activism and philanthropy work.

Marks the birthday of the bluegrass virtuoso whose angelic voice and masterful fiddle playing have made her one of country music's most respected artists. She's won 27 Grammy Awards, the most of any female artist, and has successfully bridged traditional bluegrass with contemporary country and folk. Her collaborations range from Robert Plant to Yo-Yo Ma, and she's been instrumental in keeping bluegrass music relevant to modern audiences.

Celebrates the birthday of the innovative British guitarist whose legato technique and complex harmonic approach influenced countless fusion and progressive rock musicians. His unique use of the SynthAxe and unconventional chord voicings created a instantly recognizable sound that influenced players like Frank Zappa, Eddie Van Halen, and John McLaughlin. Though he worked primarily in jazz fusion, his technical innovations and musical concepts transcended genre boundaries throughout his career.

Marks the birthday of the Allman Brothers Band's longtime drummer whose powerful, steady playing anchored the legendary Southern rock band for over four decades. Born Claude Hudson Trucks Jr., he was one of the group's founding members and helped create their distinctive dual-drummer sound alongside Jaimoe. His rock-solid foundation supported the band's improvisational jams and blues-rock explorations, making him an integral part of their enduring legacy.

Event: Amos Lee and Bruce Hornsby at Sprint Pavilion
Notes: Upcoming client show

Celebrates the birthday of the British soul singer whose powerful voice and retro sound brought traditional R&B back to mainstream prominence in the 2000s. Her album Back to Black became one of the UK's best-selling albums ever and won five Grammy Awards, featuring classics like "Rehab" and "Valerie." Despite her tragically short career, her influence on contemporary soul and jazz music continues to resonate with artists worldwide.
Amy's debut album named after Frank Sinatra

Born Andrew Warhola, celebrates his birthday today as one of the most influential artists of the 20th century. He revolutionized both visual art and music culture through his Pop Art movement and his role as producer and patron of The Velvet Underground. His iconic album covers, including the famous banana design for The Velvet Underground & Nico, remain some of the most recognizable in music history.

Marks the birthday of the Detroit-born R&B legend who became known as the Queen of Quiet Storm. Her breakthrough album Rapture in 1986 produced hits like "Sweet Love" and "Caught Up in the Rapture," earning her multiple Grammy Awards. Her distinctive contralto voice and sophisticated approach to soul music influenced a generation of singers.

Marks the release of what many consider Anthrax's masterpiece and a thrash metal classic. The album peaked at number 62 on the Billboard 200 and featured politically charged tracks like "Indians" and "I Am the Law," inspired by the comic book character Judge Dredd. Producer Eddie Kramer captured the band's tight performance and socially conscious lyrics at their creative peak.

Event: Sound of White Noise album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of Anthrax's sixth studio album, their first with new vocalist John Bush replacing Joey Belladonna. The album marked a significant shift toward a heavier, more alternative metal sound and reached number 7 on the Billboard 200. Tracks like "Only" and the title track showcased the band's evolution during the grunge era.

Marks the release of Anthrax's fourth studio album, the follow-up to their acclaimed Among the Living. The album reached number 30 on the Billboard 200 and featured the singles "Antisocial" and "Make Me Laugh." Despite mixed critical reception, it demonstrated the band's continued evolution within the thrash metal genre.

Celebrates the birthday of Anthrax's original and longtime vocalist, born Joseph Bellardini in Oswego, New York. He fronted the band during their most successful period from 1984 to 1992, including classic albums like Among the Living and Persistence of Time. His operatic vocal style became a defining characteristic of Anthrax's sound during the height of the thrash metal movement.

Marks the birthday of Anthrax's drummer and primary songwriter, born in the Bronx. He joined the band in 1983 and became the driving force behind their rhythmic intensity and songwriting direction. His drumming style helped define the thrash metal genre, and he also contributed guitar work and backing vocals throughout the band's career.

Celebrates the birthday of Anthrax's rhythm guitarist and founding member, born Scott Ian Rosenfeld in Queens. He formed the band in 1981 and became known for his distinctive palm-muted guitar style and energetic stage presence. His songwriting and leadership helped establish Anthrax as one of the "Big Four" thrash metal bands alongside Metallica, Megadeth, and Slayer.

Marks the birthday of the Montreal-born musician, singer, and multi-instrumentalist who co-founded Arcade Fire with her husband Win Butler. She contributes vocals, keyboards, accordion, and various percussion instruments to the band's orchestral indie rock sound. Her Haitian heritage and artistic vision have been integral to the band's Grammy-winning success.

Celebrates the release of Franklin's eleventh studio album, which continued her incredible streak following her Atlantic Records breakthrough. The album reached number 2 on the R&B charts and featured the hit single "Chain of Fools," which became another signature song. Recorded at Atlantic's New York studios, it solidified her position as the undisputed Queen of Soul.

Event: I Never Loved a Man the Way I Love You album released
Notes: Marks the release of the album that established Franklin as the Queen of Soul and launched her legendary Atlantic Records era. The title track became her first number 1 R&B hit, while "Respect" transformed Otis Redding's song into a feminist and civil rights anthem. Recorded at Fame Studios in Muscle Shoals, Alabama, the album reached number 2 on the Billboard 200.

Event: The Electrifying Aretha Franklin album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of Franklin's third album for Columbia Records, showcasing her powerful gospel-trained voice in a jazz and blues context. While it didn't achieve the commercial success of her later Atlantic work, the album demonstrated her versatility and vocal prowess. The record featured standards and showcased the raw talent that would soon make her a household name.

Marks the birthday of the Detroit-born daughter of gospel legend C.L. Franklin, who became the undisputed Queen of Soul. Her career spanned over five decades, producing classics like "Respect," "Think," and "Natural Woman" while earning 18 Grammy Awards. She was the first woman inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame and received the Presidential Medal of Freedom.

Celebrates the birthday of the legendary session keyboardist and songwriter who played on many of Franklin's Atlantic Records classics. Born in Center Star, Alabama, he was a key member of the Muscle Shoals Rhythm Section and played piano on hits like "I Never Loved a Man." His soulful playing contributed to the signature sound of countless R&B and soul recordings.

Marks the release of Franklin's fourth Atlantic album, continuing her remarkable hot streak of the late 1960s. The album featured the hit "Think," which became one of her most enduring anthems and later gained renewed popularity in the film The Blues Brothers. The record reached number 5 on the R&B charts and showcased her continued evolution as an artist.

Celebrates the release of Franklin's third Atlantic album, following her breakthrough success with "Respect." The album reached number 5 on the Billboard 200 and featured hits like "Baby I Love You" and "A Natural Woman." The record continued to showcase the powerful chemistry between Franklin and Atlantic's production team.

Event: Spirit In The Dark album released
Notes: Marks the release of Franklin's seventh Atlantic studio album, featuring a return to her gospel roots alongside contemporary soul. The title track became a moderate hit, and the album showcased her powerful vocal delivery over funky, horn-driven arrangements. The record demonstrated Franklin's ability to evolve with changing musical trends while maintaining her distinctive style.

Celebrates the birthday of the Boca Raton-born pop superstar who transitioned from Nickelodeon actress to global music sensation. Her albums like "Thank U, Next" and "Positions" have topped charts worldwide, while her four-octave vocal range draws comparisons to Mariah Carey. She has become one of the most streamed artists of the digital era and a major influence on contemporary pop music.

Marks the birthday of the Forest Hills-born singer best known as half of the legendary folk duo Simon and Garfunkel. His distinctive tenor harmonies helped create classics like "The Sound of Silence," "Mrs. Robinson," and "Bridge Over Troubled Water." After the duo's split, he pursued a successful solo career and maintained his reputation as one of popular music's most recognizable voices.

Event: Asia goes #1 on US album charts
Notes: Went on to become the best selling US album of 1982
Their third studio album

Event: Treasure Epilogue: Action to Answer album released
Notes: This marks the anniversary of the repackaged album that concluded the group's inaugural Treasure series. The release featured the hit title track "Answer" and showcased the octet's storytelling prowess across their conceptual universe. The album solidified their position as fourth-generation K-pop leaders before transitioning into their Fever era.

Event: Treasure EP.2: Zero to One album released
Notes: This celebrates the release of the group's second mini-album, which continued building their pirate-themed narrative established in their debut. The EP included the powerful title track "Say My Name" and helped establish their international fanbase. This release demonstrated their rapid artistic growth just months after debut.

Event: Treasure EP. Map to Answer album released
Notes: This marks the anniversary of another compilation release that bridged their Treasure and Fever eras. The album collected previous tracks while adding new material, including collaborations and alternate versions. This release helped consolidate their early discography for fans discovering the group's evolving sound.

This celebrates the release that continued their Fever trilogy with the explosive title track "Fireworks (I'm The One)". The album showcased their matured sound and included fan-favorite B-sides that demonstrated their versatility. This release further cemented their reputation for high-energy performances and conceptual storytelling.

Today marks the birthday of Yunho, the group's main dancer and vocalist known for his exceptional stage presence and charismatic performances. Born Jeong Yun-ho, he serves as one of the group's key performers and has been praised for his dance skills and energetic personality. His contributions to the group's choreography and visual storytelling have been instrumental to their success.

Today celebrates the birthday of Seonghwa, the group's vocalist and visual who is known for his striking stage presence and artistic sensibility. Born Park Seong-hwa, he often takes on leadership roles in performances and has been recognized for his fashion sense and creative input. His dual nature of elegant visuals and powerful stage charisma has made him a standout member.

This marks the anniversary of their recent album that showcased a more mature and refined sound as the group entered their sixth year. The release demonstrated their continued evolution as artists while maintaining the energetic performances fans expect. The album received strong international chart performance and critical acclaim for its production quality.

Event: Treasure EP. 3: One to All album released
Notes: This celebrates the third installment of their Treasure EP series, which included the hit title track "Wave" that showed a different, more summery side to their sound. The album demonstrated their versatility beyond their typical intense concepts and became a fan favorite for its refreshing energy. This release helped expand their musical range during their rookie year.

Today marks the birthday of Yeosang, the group's vocalist and visual known for his unique tone and striking appearance. Born Kang Yeo-sang, he brings a distinctive vocal color to the group's harmonies and has been praised for his improved stage confidence over the years. His evolution as a performer has been particularly noted by fans and critics alike.

Event: The World EP. 2: Outlaw album released
Notes: This marks the anniversary of their album that continued The World series with darker, more intense themes and the powerful title track "Bouncy (K-Hot Chilli Pepper)". The release showcased their growth as performers and storytellers while maintaining their signature high-energy style. The album achieved strong international chart positions and demonstrated their global appeal.

Today celebrates the birthday of San, one of the group's main dancers and vocalists renowned for his intense stage presence and exceptional performance skills. Born Choi San, he's often praised for his facial expressions and ability to embody different concepts completely. His passionate performances and dedication to his craft have made him one of K-pop's most recognized performers.

This celebrates the beginning of their Fever era with an album that marked a significant artistic evolution from their Treasure series. The title track "Inception" showcased a more sophisticated sound and complex choreography that elevated their status in the industry. This release is often cited as a turning point in their career trajectory.

Event: The World EP.1: Movement album released
Notes: This marks the anniversary of the album that launched The World series and demonstrated their continued artistic growth three years post-debut. The release featured the title track "Guerrilla" and showcased more mature themes and complex musical arrangements. The album received critical acclaim for its cohesive concept and strong performance tracks.

Today marks the birthday of Mingi, the group's main rapper known for his deep voice and charismatic rap delivery. Born Song Min-gi, he's recognized for his songwriting contributions and distinctive flow that adds depth to the group's tracks. His return to activities after a hiatus was celebrated by fans worldwide, highlighting his importance to the group's dynamic.

This celebrates the conclusion of their successful Fever trilogy, which included the hit title track "Deja Vu" that showcased their most mature sound to date. The album demonstrated their artistic growth and ability to tackle complex themes while maintaining their signature energy. This release solidified their position as leaders of the fourth generation of K-pop.

Event: Treasure EP. FIN: All to Action album released
Notes: This marks the anniversary of the album that concluded their debut Treasure series with the powerful title track "Wonderland". The release demonstrated their storytelling abilities and featured some of their most ambitious choreography and production work. This album established many of the theatrical and conceptual elements that would become their signature style.

Today celebrates the birthday of Jongho, the group's main vocalist and youngest member known for his powerful vocals and impressive high notes. Born Choi Jong-ho, he serves as the vocal anchor of the group and has been praised for his technical ability and stage presence despite being the maknae. His vocal performances are often highlighted as among the strongest in fourth-generation K-pop.

Event: Treasure EP.1: All to Zero album released
Notes: This marks the anniversary of their groundbreaking debut album that introduced the world to their pirate concept and intense performance style. The debut title track "Pirate King" established their theatrical approach and high-energy performances that would become their trademark. This release launched one of fourth-generation K-pop's most successful acts and began their journey to global recognition.

Born Kim Hong-joong, he serves as the leader, rapper, and producer of the internationally successful K-pop group ATEEZ. His leadership and creative vision helped guide the group to multiple Billboard 200 chart appearances and a dedicated global fanbase. Hong-joong is particularly known for his hands-on involvement in the group's music production and his distinctive fashion sense that has influenced K-pop styling trends.

Born Jung Woo-young, he performs as a main dancer, vocalist, and rapper in ATEEZ, bringing exceptional choreographic skills and stage charisma to the group. His dance abilities have been widely praised in the K-pop community, helping establish ATEEZ as one of the most performance-focused fourth-generation groups. Woo-young's versatility across singing, rapping, and dancing makes him one of the group's most well-rounded performers.

Event: The World EP. FIN: Will album released
Notes: This album marked the conclusion of ATEEZ's ambitious "The World" series, showcasing their evolution as both performers and storytellers. The release demonstrated the group's growing international appeal, featuring tracks that blend their signature intense performance style with more mature musical arrangements. The album continued their streak of Billboard 200 success, solidifying their position among the top fourth-generation K-pop acts globally.

Event: Treasure EP. Extra: Shift the Map album released
Notes: This EP release came during ATEEZ's breakthrough year, featuring the hit track "Answer" which became one of their most iconic songs. The album helped establish their reputation for cinematic music videos and complex choreography that would become their trademark. Released just over a year after their debut, it demonstrated their rapid artistic growth and growing international fanbase.

This release concluded ATEEZ's "Zero: Fever" series, which had been exploring themes of youth, rebellion, and self-discovery throughout multiple installments. The album featured some of their most introspective work alongside their characteristic high-energy tracks, showing artistic maturity. It marked another milestone in their journey toward becoming one of K-pop's most consistent chart performers internationally.

This quirky album from the experimental banjo virtuoso showcased Barnes' unique blend of bluegrass, electronic music, and avant-garde sensibilities. Known for his work with the Bad Livers, Barnes created a deliberately lo-fi collection that challenged traditional genre boundaries. The album exemplified ATO Records' commitment to supporting artists who push musical conventions in unexpected directions.

This collaborative album saw the renowned acoustic guitar duo team up with a full Cuban orchestra, creating a stunning fusion of flamenco-influenced guitar work and Latin jazz arrangements. Recorded in Havana, the project expanded their sound beyond their typical instrumental approach while maintaining their signature interplay. The album received critical acclaim for its ambitious cross-cultural musical dialogue and virtuosic performances.

This singer-songwriter and multi-instrumentalist is known for her raw, emotionally direct indie rock approach and impressive guitar skills. Her albums "Emotions and Math" and "Devotion" established her as a compelling voice in contemporary indie music, blending introspective lyrics with bold musical arrangements. Glaspy's honest songwriting and powerful live performances have earned critical praise and a dedicated following in the indie music scene.

This album from the Athens, Georgia rock trio showcased their evolution from garage rock origins toward a more polished, arena-ready sound. The record featured driving guitar work and anthemic choruses that helped establish them in the Southern rock revival movement of the late 2000s. Mission Control demonstrated the band's ability to craft both intimate songs and bigger rock statements.

The guitarist and multi-instrumentalist is best known as a member of My Morning Jacket, where his versatile playing has contributed to the band's expansive sound across multiple acclaimed albums. Broemel has also released solo work that showcases his skills across guitar, pedal steel, and saxophone, demonstrating his wide-ranging musical abilities. His contributions to My Morning Jacket's live performances have been particularly noted for their improvisational elements and musical variety.

This reggae-rock album from the Virginia-based band continued their exploration of socially conscious themes over laid-back grooves and melodic hooks. The record featured collaborations with several notable reggae artists and addressed topics of personal and social resilience. SOJA's accessible approach to reggae-influenced rock helped them build a substantial following in the American reggae scene.

The bassist and backing vocalist has been a core member of the British indie rock band Gomez since their formation in the mid-1990s. Gray contributed to the band's unique sound that blended blues, rock, and electronic elements across multiple albums including their Mercury Prize-winning debut "Bring It On." His bass work provided the rhythmic foundation for Gomez's eclectic and critically acclaimed catalog.

Event: Two Gallants "We Are Undone" album released
Notes: This album marked a return for the San Francisco folk-rock duo after a brief hiatus, featuring their characteristic blend of acoustic guitars, harmonies, and introspective songwriting. The record showcased Adam Stephens and Tyson Vogel's continued evolution as songwriters while maintaining the intimate, raw quality that originally drew attention to their work. The album demonstrated their ability to create emotionally resonant music with minimal instrumentation.

Event: Les Claypool's Duo De Twang "Four Foot Shack" album released
Notes: This country-tinged project from the Primus bassist showed Claypool exploring roots music with collaborator Bernie Worrell and other musicians. The album featured traditional country and bluegrass influences filtered through Claypool's distinctive bass playing and quirky songwriting perspective. The collaboration represented another example of Claypool's willingness to experiment across genres while maintaining his unique musical identity.

Event: Jim James "Regions of Light and Sounds of God" album released
Notes: This solo debut from the My Morning Jacket frontman showcased a more experimental and introspective side of his artistry, featuring layered vocals and atmospheric production. The album explored themes of spirituality and personal reflection through a dreamy, reverb-heavy sonic landscape. James' solo work demonstrated his abilities beyond the rock context of his main band, earning critical praise for its ambitious scope and emotional depth.

This mariachi-influenced album from The Bronx's alter ego project continued their exploration of traditional Mexican music filtered through punk rock sensibilities. The record featured authentic mariachi instrumentation and arrangements while maintaining the energy and attitude of their hardcore punk origins. The project demonstrated the band's versatility and respect for mariachi traditions while creating something genuinely unique.

This EP from the Nashville singer-songwriter showcased his evolution from folk-rock toward a more polished, radio-friendly sound that would characterize his later work. The release featured Baxter's warm vocals and thoughtful songwriting over carefully crafted arrangements that drew from both indie rock and Americana traditions. The EP helped establish him as an emerging voice in the contemporary Nashville music scene.

Event: Patty Griffin - Children Running Through album released
Notes: This album from the acclaimed singer-songwriter continued her exploration of deeply personal themes through her distinctive, powerful vocals and introspective songwriting. Griffin's work on the record demonstrated her ability to address difficult subjects with both vulnerability and strength, earning critical praise for its emotional honesty.
