His expressive single-string solos and vibrato technique with his beloved guitar Lucille inspired countless rock and blues musicians, while songs like 'The Thrill Is Gone' brought blues music to mainstream audiences worldwide.

This lyricist and jingle writer became an essential creative partner to Brian Wilson during The Beach Boys' most ambitious period. Asher co-wrote the lyrics for the groundbreaking 'Pet Sounds' album in 1966, helping craft some of the most sophisticated and emotionally complex songs in the band's catalog including 'Wouldn't It Be Nice' and 'God Only Knows.'

This album captured The Beach Boys at their surf rock peak, reaching number 4 on the Billboard 200 and spawning the hit singles 'I Get Around' and 'Wendy.' Recorded during their most prolific touring period, the collection perfectly encapsulated the California summer lifestyle that Brian Wilson and the band had made their signature sound.

Event: Licensed To Ill goes #1 on US album charts
Notes: The Beastie Boys became the first rap act to have a No.1 album in the US with their debut album, Licensed To Ill.

This album marked the trio's creative renaissance, debuting at number 1 and earning them three Grammy Awards including Best Alternative Music Performance. Recorded over two years, the album featured the hit single 'Intergalactic' and showcased their return to innovative sampling techniques and playful lyricism after a period of legal battles and creative uncertainty.

Initially considered a commercial disappointment, this album is now hailed as a masterpiece of sampling and hip-hop production. Created with the Dust Brothers, the record's dense layers of obscure samples and pop culture references influenced countless producers, though legal issues surrounding its innovative sampling techniques made similar approaches financially prohibitive for future releases.

Born Adam Nathaniel Yauch, he served as the bassist and one of three MCs in the groundbreaking hip-hop trio. Beyond music, MCA became known for his activism, particularly regarding Tibetan independence, and later directed several of the group's innovative music videos under the pseudonym Nathanial Hornblower before his death from cancer in 2012.

Born Adam Keefe Horovitz, he joined the group as guitarist when they transitioned from hardcore punk to hip-hop in the mid-1980s. The son of playwright Israel Horovitz, Ad-Rock became known for his distinctive nasal vocal style and remained with the group throughout their entire career, continuing to tour and record after MCA's passing.

This debut album became the first rap record to top the Billboard 200, launching hip-hop into the mainstream and selling over 10 million copies worldwide. Featuring the anthem 'Fight For Your Right,' the album's rock-influenced production and rebellious attitude made the Brooklyn trio unlikely MTV stars and opened doors for rap music on rock radio stations.

Born Michael Louis Diamond, he co-founded the group as a hardcore punk band in 1981 before their pivotal shift to hip-hop. Known for his rapid-fire delivery and beatboxing skills, Mike D helped steer the group's evolution from New York punk rockers to globally influential hip-hop pioneers, remaining active in music and fashion ventures throughout his career.

Born Bek David Campbell and later known as Beck Hansen, he became one of alternative rock's most innovative and unpredictable artists. His breakthrough single 'Loser' in 1993 established him as a pioneer of anti-folk and alternative hip-hop, while subsequent albums like 'Odelay' and 'Sea Change' showcased his ability to masterfully blend genres from folk to electronic music.

This introspective album marked a dramatic departure from Beck's previous experimental work, featuring primarily acoustic arrangements and deeply personal lyrics about heartbreak. The record earned widespread critical acclaim and a Grammy nomination for Album of the Year, with standout tracks like 'Lost Cause' and 'Golden Age' showcasing his songwriting maturity and Nigel Godrich's subtle production.

Born Barry Alan Crompton Gibb on the Isle of Man, he became the eldest and surviving member of the legendary pop trio. As the group's primary songwriter and distinctive falsetto voice behind disco classics like 'Stayin' Alive' and 'How Deep Is Your Love,' Barry helped define the sound of the 1970s and wrote numerous hits for other artists throughout his career.

Born as twins Robin Hugh and Maurice Ernest Gibb, they formed the core of the Bee Gees alongside older brother Barry. Robin's vibrato-heavy lead vocals powered early hits like 'Massachusetts,' while Maurice's multi-instrumental skills and harmonies were essential to their later disco success, until Maurice's death in 2003 and Robin's in 2012.
Store #2 at Musictoday!

The former Go-Go's lead singer achieved massive solo success in the late 1980s with hits like 'Mad About You' and 'Heaven Is a Place on Earth.' Her transition from new wave punk pioneer to mainstream pop star demonstrated her versatility as a performer, while her work with the Go-Go's helped establish all-female rock bands as commercially viable acts.

This debut album from the New Edition spinoff trio marks a defining moment in new jack swing history. The title track became a massive hit, reaching number 3 on the Billboard Hot 100 and spawning countless covers and samples. Producer Dr. Freeze helped craft a sound that perfectly captured the transition from 80s R&B to 90s hip-hop soul.

Event: Whatever and Ever Amen album released
Notes: This second Ben Folds Five album solidifies their reputation as masters of piano-driven alternative rock. The record spawned the hit single "Brick," a deeply personal song about teenage pregnancy that reached number 6 on the Modern Rock charts. The album's blend of vulnerability and wit established Folds as one of the 90s' most distinctive songwriters.

This B-sides and rarities collection celebrates Folds' prolific output during his solo career transition. The compilation features alternate versions, unreleased tracks, and live recordings that showcase his experimental side. It serves as a bridge between his Ben Folds Five era and his emerging solo identity.

Event: Complete Best of Ben Folds Five and Ben Folds
Notes: This comprehensive retrospective marks the first career-spanning collection covering both Ben Folds Five and his solo work. The compilation traces his evolution from indie piano rock pioneer to accomplished solo artist. It includes remastered versions of fan favorites alongside rare tracks from both phases of his career.

Event: Solo - The Sound of Last Night... This Morning
Notes: This intimate acoustic album captures Folds performing alone at the piano in a Nashville studio. Recorded in a single night, it features stripped-down versions of songs from across his catalog. The spontaneous nature of the recording highlights his skills as both performer and arranger.

This third solo studio album finds Folds exploring more personal territory with sophisticated arrangements. The record features collaborations with a string section and guest musicians, expanding his sonic palette. Songs like "Landed" and "Gracie" showcase his ability to balance humor with genuine emotion.

Event: The Unauthorized Biography of Reinhold Messner album released
Notes: This final Ben Folds Five album represents the culmination of their piano rock experimentation. Despite its commercial underperformance compared to previous releases, it contains some of their most adventurous songwriting. The album's title references the famous mountaineer, reflecting themes of ambition and perseverance.

This marriage to Australian artist Frally Hynes marks a significant personal milestone during Folds' creative peak. The relationship influenced several songs on his subsequent releases, including references to Australia and long-distance relationships. Their union lasted until 2006 and produced two children.

This companion EP to Songs for Silverman features alternate versions and B-sides from those recording sessions. The collection includes covers and experimental tracks that didn't make the main album. It demonstrates Folds' prolific songwriting during this period of his solo career.

Event: Played his first completely solo show in years at Western Connecticut State University
Notes: This intimate performance marks a return to his roots as a solo piano performer after years of full-band shows. The concert at the small Connecticut venue allowed fans to experience his songs in their most essential form. The show was part of his transition back to more acoustic-focused performances.

Event: Reunited with Ben Folds Five to play the Mountain Jam Music Festival (first time since 2008)
Notes: This festival appearance marks the trio's first reunion performance in four years, thrilling longtime fans. The Mountain Jam Festival performance tested the waters for what would become a full-scale Ben Folds Five reunion. The positive response led to new recordings and a proper reunion tour later that year.

Event: First of their two twins (Louis Francis) born
Notes: The birth of Louis Francis represents the first of twins born to Folds and his wife Frally Hynes. This major life event influenced his songwriting significantly, with fatherhood themes appearing throughout his subsequent work. The experience of having twins inspired several touching songs about family and responsibility.

This EP showcases Folds' continued experimentation with different recording techniques and song arrangements. The release features covers alongside original material, highlighting his interpretive skills. It serves as a creative palette cleanser between major album releases.

Event: Second of their two twins (Gracie Scott) born
Notes: Gracie Scott's birth completes the arrival of Folds' twin children, born just one day after her brother Louis. The experience of sudden fatherhood to twins profoundly impacted his perspective and songwriting. Gracie later inspired the touching song "Gracie" on his Songs for Silverman album.

This self-titled debut album introduces Ben Folds Five's unique piano-bass-drums lineup to the alternative rock scene. Despite having no guitar, the trio created a full, energetic sound that stood out among grunge-dominated 90s music. The album's clever wordplay and musical sophistication earned critical acclaim and a devoted fanbase.

Event: Received a star on the Music City Walk of Fame in Nashville, TN
Notes: This honor recognizes Folds' significant contributions to Nashville's music scene as both resident and frequent collaborator. The star celebrates his work with country artists and his role in the city's diverse musical community. The ceremony acknowledges his crossover appeal beyond alternative rock into multiple genres.

This EP continues Folds' tradition of releasing experimental material between major albums. The collection features alternate takes and covers that showcase his range as both songwriter and interpreter. It demonstrates his prolific output during his most active solo period.

This iTunes exclusive release combines live performances, studio tracks, and interview segments in the platform's signature format. The album provides insight into Folds' creative process and personal history through both music and spoken word. It represents an early example of artist-specific digital content creation.

This debut solo album establishes Folds as a formidable artist beyond Ben Folds Five, featuring the hit single "Rockin' the Suburbs." Released on September 11th, the album's themes of suburban alienation took on unexpected resonance given the day's tragic events. The record successfully translates his piano rock vision to a solo context with full band arrangements.

Born Benjamin Scott Folds in Winston-Salem, North Carolina, he celebrates another year as one of alternative rock's most distinctive voices. His piano-driven approach revolutionized 90s alternative music with Ben Folds Five before launching a successful solo career. Beyond music, he's served as a judge on NBC's The Sing-Off and continues touring internationally as both performer and conductor.

Celebrates the release of his third solo studio album, which marked a return to more rock-oriented sounds after the orchestral experiments of his previous work. The album featured collaborations with producer Joe Chiccarelli and included tracks like "You Don't Know Me" featuring Regina Spektor, showcasing his continued evolution as a solo artist following his Ben Folds Five success.

Event: BFF - The Sound of the Life of the Mind
Notes: Marks the release of his collaboration album with contemporary classical ensemble yMusic, blending his piano-driven songwriting with chamber music arrangements. The experimental project demonstrated his ongoing interest in merging pop sensibilities with orchestral elements, featuring intricate compositions that pushed beyond traditional singer-songwriter boundaries.

Commemorates the release of this four-track EP that served as a bridge between his Ben Folds Five era and solo career development. The release showcased his intimate piano-based compositions and helped establish his credentials as a solo performer, featuring the contemplative title track and fan favorite "Learn to Live with What You Are."

Celebrates his first official live solo album, capturing performances from his early solo touring period after Ben Folds Five's initial breakup. The album demonstrated his ability to translate his piano rock anthems to an intimate solo setting, featuring crowd favorites and showcasing the wit and musicianship that made him a compelling live performer.

Event: Motor City album released (Live at Detroit, MI 10/9/08)
Notes: Marks the release of this live recording captured during his solo tour, showcasing his dynamic stage presence and piano virtuosity. The Detroit performance highlighted his ability to connect with audiences through both his Ben Folds Five catalog and solo material, demonstrating the energy and spontaneity that made his live shows essential experiences for fans.

Commemorates the release of this rarities collection featuring 55 unreleased tracks, demos, and alternate versions spanning his career. The comprehensive vault release gave fans unprecedented access to his creative process, including early songwriting experiments and alternate arrangements that provided insight into his artistic development.

Event: Compilation - The Best Imitation of Myself: A Retrospective
Notes: Celebrates this career-spanning retrospective that compiled his most significant solo work and Ben Folds Five tracks into a definitive collection. The compilation served as both an introduction for new listeners and a comprehensive overview for longtime fans, highlighting his evolution from alternative rock piano man to respected solo artist and composer.

Marks another live recording from his solo tour, capturing the intimate connection between performer and audience that defined his concert experiences. The Dallas show represented his continued documentation of live performances, preserving the spontaneous moments and audience interactions that made each concert unique.

Commemorates this compilation of rarities, B-sides, and unreleased material that provided fans with deeper access to his creative output. The collection highlighted his prolific songwriting and willingness to experiment, featuring tracks that didn't fit traditional album formats but showcased his diverse musical interests and piano-driven aesthetic.

Marks the end of his marriage to Frally Hynes, reflecting the personal transitions that often influenced his introspective songwriting. His relationships and personal experiences frequently provided material for his emotionally honest compositions, contributing to the authentic vulnerability that resonated with his audience.

Event: Married Fleur Stanbrook @ the Venetian Hotel in Las Vegas, NV
Notes: Celebrates his marriage to Fleur Stanbrook in a Las Vegas ceremony, marking a new chapter in his personal life. The wedding took place during a particularly productive period in his solo career, as he continued to tour extensively and develop his post-Ben Folds Five artistic identity.

Commemorates the first volume of this comprehensive archival series, covering his early career including Ben Folds Five's formation and breakthrough years. The collection provided fans with rare glimpses into his musical development, featuring demos and alternate versions from the period that established him as an innovative voice in alternative rock.

Marks the second installment of his vault series, spanning the peak Ben Folds Five years and early solo transition period. This volume captured his artistic evolution during a crucial time when he was moving from band leader to solo performer, including rarities from both his group and individual work.

Celebrates the final volume of his comprehensive vault series, covering his established solo career and artistic maturation. The collection documented his growth as a composer and performer, including collaborations and experiments that showed his expanding musical horizons beyond traditional piano rock.

Marks this intimate live recording captured at the iconic music retail venue, showcasing his ability to perform in stripped-down acoustic settings. The Tower Records performance highlighted his piano skills and vocal delivery in an up-close environment, demonstrating the versatility that made him equally compelling in both large venues and intimate spaces.

Commemorates the end of his marriage to Fleur Stanbrook after several years together, marking another personal transition in his life. These personal experiences continued to influence his songwriting, as he channeled life changes into the honest, emotionally resonant compositions that defined his artistic voice.

Born Benjamin Chase Harper in Pomona, California, he became one of the most socially conscious singer-songwriters of his generation, blending folk, blues, and rock with masterful slide guitar work. His breakthrough album "Fight for Your Mind" and collaborations with groups like the Innocent Criminals established him as both a virtuoso musician and passionate activist, earning multiple Grammy Awards while consistently addressing social justice issues through his music.

Event: Take My Breath Away #1 on US singles
Notes: Marks the chart-topping success of the synth-pop group's signature ballad, which served as the love theme for "Top Gun" and became one of the defining songs of the 1980s. The track's success revitalized the band's career and became synonymous with the blockbuster film, earning an Academy Award for Best Original Song and cementing its place in pop culture history.

Born in Honolulu, Hawaii, she became one of America's most versatile entertainers, conquering music, film, and Broadway with her powerful voice and comedic timing. From her early days performing in New York bathhouses to winning Grammy, Emmy, Tony, and Golden Globe awards, she established herself as a true triple threat, with hits like "The Rose" and "Wind Beneath My Wings" showcasing her ability to deliver both humor and heartfelt emotion.

Born Beyoncé Giselle Knowles in Houston, Texas, she evolved from Destiny's Child lead singer to one of the most influential artists of the 21st century, redefining pop music and visual artistry. Her groundbreaking solo albums like "Lemonade" and surprise releases revolutionized music distribution while addressing themes of feminism, race, and empowerment, earning her the most Grammy Awards of any artist in history and establishing her as a global cultural icon.

Event: I Am Sasha Fierce goes #1 on US album charts
Notes: It debuted at No.1, making her the 3rd female artist this decade (along with Britney Spears and Alicia Keys) to have her first three albums debut in the top spot.

Today marks the birth of John Rich, who became half of the country music duo Big & Rich alongside Big Kenny. The pair achieved massive success with their 2004 debut album Horse of a Different Color, featuring the hit 'Save a Horse (Ride a Cowboy).' Rich also found success as a songwriter, penning hits for other country artists before forming the duo.

Today celebrates the birth of Antwan André Patton, better known as Big Boi, one half of the groundbreaking hip-hop duo OutKast. Along with André 3000, he helped revolutionize Southern hip-hop with albums like Aquemini and Stankonia, bringing Atlanta's music scene to global prominence. His distinctive rapid-fire delivery and production skills made OutKast one of the most innovative acts in hip-hop history.

Store Name: Big Brother and the Holding Company
Notes: Today marks the birth of James Gurley, the innovative guitarist for Big Brother and the Holding Company who helped define the psychedelic rock sound of the late 1960s. His experimental, feedback-heavy guitar work provided the sonic backdrop for Janis Joplin's powerful vocals on albums like Cheap Thrills. Gurley's unconventional playing style and use of distortion influenced countless guitarists in the emerging heavy rock genre.

This date marks the release of Capital Punishment, the debut album by Big Pun that made him the first Latino rapper to achieve platinum status. The album peaked at number 5 on the Billboard 200 and featured the hit single 'Still Not a Player,' showcasing Big Pun's complex wordplay and rapid-fire delivery. Recorded in the Bronx, the album established him as one of the most skilled lyricists in hip-hop before his untimely death in 2000.

Today celebrates the birth of Sean Michael Leonard Anderson, known professionally as Big Sean, in Santa Monica, California. The Detroit-raised rapper gained recognition after being discovered by Kanye West and signed to G.O.O.D. Music in 2007. He's since released multiple platinum albums including Dark Sky Paradise and achieved numerous chart-topping singles with his distinctive flow and introspective lyrics.

Event: Rock Around The Clock goes #1 on US singles charts
Notes: This date marks a pivotal moment in music history when 'Rock Around the Clock' by Bill Haley & His Comets reached number one on the US singles chart. The song's success helped launch the rock and roll era into mainstream American culture, becoming an anthem for teenage rebellion. Its prominent use in the film Blackboard Jungle earlier that year had already established it as a cultural phenomenon that would define a generation.

Event: Rock Around The Clock album released
Notes: This date marks the release of Rock Around the Clock, the album that capitalized on the massive success of Bill Haley and His Comets' groundbreaking title track. The album helped establish rock and roll as a commercial force in American music, featuring the band's energetic fusion of country, rhythm and blues, and pop. The record became one of the first rock and roll albums to achieve significant commercial success nationwide.

Today celebrates the birth of William Harrison Withers Jr., the soul legend who crafted some of the most enduring songs in popular music. Born in West Virginia, he didn't begin his recording career until age 32, but quickly produced classics like 'Ain't No Sunshine,' 'Lean on Me,' and 'Lovely Day.' His warm, conversational vocal style and deeply personal songwriting made him one of the most beloved artists of the 1970s.

Today marks the birth of Billie Eilish Pirate Baird O'Connell, who revolutionized pop music as a teenager with her distinctive whispered vocals and dark, minimalist production. She became the youngest artist to win all four major Grammy categories in a single year at age 18, following the success of her debut album When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go? Her innovative approach to pop music and visual artistry has influenced a new generation of artists.

Today celebrates the birth of Eleanora Fagan, known to the world as Billie Holiday, one of jazz's most influential and emotionally powerful vocalists. Her distinctive phrasing and ability to convey deep emotion made songs like 'Strange Fruit' and 'God Bless the Child' timeless classics. Despite facing significant personal struggles and racial discrimination, she remains one of the most important voices in American music history.
Influential drummer who has worked with Miles Davis, Mahavishnu Orchestra, James Brown, Santana, and more

Today marks the birth of Steve Stevens, the virtuoso guitarist whose innovative playing became integral to Billy Idol's sound throughout the 1980s. Born Steven Bruce Schneider, he crafted memorable riffs for hits like 'Rebel Yell' and 'White Wedding,' blending punk energy with technical prowess. His distinctive guitar work and use of effects helped define the MTV era's visual and sonic aesthetic.

Event: Dumped 600 dead fish in Faith No More's dressing room
Notes: Faith No More responded by walking on stage, naked during Idol's set.

This date marks the release of Rebel Yell, Billy Idol's breakthrough album that established him as a solo star after leaving Generation X. The album reached number 6 on the Billboard 200 and spawned MTV favorites including the title track, 'White Wedding,' and 'Eyes Without a Face.' Recorded with guitarist Steve Stevens, the album perfectly captured Idol's punk attitude with arena rock accessibility.

Today celebrates the birth of William Martin Joel, the 'Piano Man' who became one of America's best-selling recording artists. Born in the Bronx and raised on Long Island, he channeled his classical piano training into pop and rock masterpieces like 'Uptown Girl,' 'We Didn't Start the Fire,' and countless other hits. His storytelling ability and melodic craftsmanship have made him one of the most enduring singer-songwriters in popular music.
Singles include:  A Matter Of Trust, Modern Woman, This Is The Time
His ninth studio album; includes Tell Her About It, Uptown Girl, The Longest Time

This date marks the release of 52nd Street, Billy Joel's ambitious album that became the first to be commercially released on compact disc. The album reached number one on the Billboard 200 and won the Grammy for Album of the Year, featuring hits like 'My Life' and 'Big Shot.' Named after the famous New York jazz strip, it showcased Joel's sophisticated songwriting and diverse musical influences.

This date marks the release of Piano Man, Billy Joel's second studio album featuring his signature song that became his first major hit. The title track, inspired by his experiences playing piano at a Los Angeles lounge, reached the top 25 and established Joel's storytelling style. Though initially a modest commercial success, the album has become a classic, with 'Piano Man' serving as Joel's unofficial anthem throughout his career.

Born Leslie Sebastian Charles in Trinidad and Tobago, this soul and pop vocalist celebrates his birthday today. He scored massive international hits with "Caribbean Queen" and "When the Going Gets Tough, the Tough Get Going" during the 1980s. His smooth blend of R&B, pop, and Caribbean influences made him one of the decade's most distinctive voices.

Known as the "Fifth Beatle" for his keyboard contributions to the Fab Four's recordings, this gospel-trained musician celebrates his birthday today. He earned his nickname through his work on classics like "Get Back" and "Let It Be," while also enjoying solo success with "Nothing from Nothing" and "Will It Go Round in Circles." His Hammond organ and piano skills made him one of the most sought-after session musicians of his era.

Born in Flatwoods, Kentucky, this country artist celebrates his birthday today. His 1992 debut single "Achy Breaky Heart" became a crossover phenomenon, sparking a line dancing craze and helping introduce country music to mainstream pop audiences. Beyond his own recording career, he's also known as the father of pop star Miley Cyrus.

This Boston-born rock guitarist and vocalist celebrates his birthday today. He dominated early MTV with arena rock anthems like "The Stroke" and "Rock Me Tonite," though the latter's controversial video nearly derailed his career. His 1981 album "Don't Say No" established him as one of the era's premier guitar-driven rock acts.

This Brooklyn hardcore band's self-titled debut album marks its release today. The record helped establish the crossover between hardcore punk and heavy metal, featuring aggressive tracks like "Punishment" and "Urban Discipline." Their fusion of hip-hop influenced vocals with crushing metal riffs would prove influential in the development of nu-metal.

Born Björk Guðmundsdóttir in Reykjavik, Iceland, this avant-garde artist celebrates her birthday today. She first gained international recognition as lead vocalist of The Sugarcubes before launching a groundbreaking solo career with albums like "Debut" and "Homogenic." Her innovative blend of electronic music, art pop, and theatrical performance has made her one of music's most uncompromising and influential artists.

The longtime drummer for this Georgia rock band celebrates his birthday today. Steve Gorman provided the rhythmic backbone for hits like "She Talks to Angels" and "Black" throughout the band's classic lineup era. His steady, powerful drumming helped define the group's blues-rock sound during their 1990s heyday.

Zakk Wylde's heavy metal project releases this studio album today, marking another chapter in the former Ozzy Osbourne guitarist's brutal sonic assault. The record continues the band's tradition of crushing riffs and whiskey-soaked metal anthems. Songs like "Stoned and Drunk" showcase Wylde's signature blend of Black Sabbath-influenced doom and Southern rock swagger.

This follow-up album from Zakk Wylde's metal outfit marks its release today, delivering another dose of punishing riffs and bourbon-fueled mayhem. The record features tracks like "Stillborn" and "Doomsday Jesus," showcasing the band's continued evolution of their signature doom-laden sound. Wylde's guitar work remains as crushing and precise as ever throughout the album's runtime.

This social justice movement is founded today following the acquittal of George Zimmerman in the shooting death of Trayvon Martin. Created by activists Alicia Garza, Patrisse Cullors, and Opal Tometi, the movement would grow into a global network advocating against violence and systemic racism toward black people. The organization has significantly influenced contemporary music and culture, with countless artists incorporating its message into their work.

The legendary metal pioneers play their final concert today in Birmingham, England, bringing their groundbreaking 49-year career to a close. The emotional farewell show at the NEC Arena featured original members Ozzy Osbourne, Tony Iommi, and Geezer Butler performing classics like "Paranoid" and "Iron Man." The concert marked the end of "The End" tour and concluded one of heavy metal's most influential legacies.

Event: Black Sabbath album released in UK
Notes: Released on Friday, the 13th - and so begins heavy metal

The legendary guitarist and primary songwriter celebrates his birthday today in Birmingham, England. Tony Iommi's innovative heavy riffs, created despite losing fingertips in a factory accident, essentially invented heavy metal with songs like "Iron Man" and "War Pigs." His dark, crushing guitar tone and innovative use of downtuned instruments became the blueprint for generations of metal musicians.

The original drummer celebrates his birthday today in Birmingham, England. Bill Ward's thunderous, jazz-influenced drumming provided the rhythmic foundation for heavy metal classics like "Paranoid" and "Sweet Leaf." His powerful, dynamic style helped define the sound of heavy metal drumming and influenced countless musicians in the genre.

The band's nineteenth studio album marks its release today, reuniting the three surviving original members after 35 years. Produced by Rick Rubin, "13" features Ozzy Osbourne, Tony Iommi, and Geezer Butler crafting their first new material together since 1978's "Never Say Die!" The album topped charts worldwide and proved the metal legends could still create compelling music in their seventh decade.
Sang with Elf, Rainbow, Dio, and Black Sabbath

The bass guitarist and primary lyricist celebrates his birthday today in Birmingham, England. Geezer Butler's heavy, innovative bass lines and dark lyrical themes helped create heavy metal's foundation on classics like "N.I.B." and "Children of the Grave." His bass work and songwriting contributions were essential to defining the band's revolutionary sound and metal's lyrical themes.

This landmark third album marks its release today, cementing the band's role as heavy metal's founding fathers. "Master of Reality" features downtuned guitars and classics like "Sweet Leaf" and "Children of the Grave," showcasing Tony Iommi's increasingly heavy sound. The album's shorter, more focused songs and crushing riffs influenced countless metal bands and remains a genre masterpiece.

This sixth studio album marks its release today during a period of internal band turmoil and alleged record label interference. Despite the chaos, "Sabotage" produced classics like "Symptom of the Universe" and "The Writ," showcasing the band's continued musical evolution. The album's heavier sound and complex arrangements demonstrated their resilience and continued creativity amid personal struggles.

This groundbreaking second album marks its release today, essentially creating the template for heavy metal music. "Paranoid" features immortal classics like the title track, "Iron Man," and "War Pigs," establishing the band as the godfathers of metal. Recorded in just four days, the album's crushing riffs and dark themes would influence generations of musicians and define an entire genre.

Today marks the 52nd anniversary of this pivotal fourth album that reached number 8 on the Billboard 200 and showcased the band's experimentation with synthesizers and orchestration. The record featured classics like "Tomorrow's Dream" and "Snowblind" while exploring themes of cocaine use and alienation. Recorded during the height of their substance abuse issues, the album demonstrated Black Sabbath's willingness to evolve beyond their heavy metal blueprint.

Today marks the 48th anniversary of this controversial seventh studio album that peaked at number 13 in the UK despite mixed reviews from critics and fans. The record featured a more experimental approach with tracks like "It's Alright" showcasing synthesizers and mellotrons, marking a significant departure from their signature heavy sound. Internal tensions and heavy drug use during recording sessions contributed to the album's uneven reception and foreshadowed lineup changes ahead.
All four original members of Black Sabbath reunited momentarily to play 'Paranoid' on US TV's David Letterman Show.

Event: Black Sabbath announce reunion and new album
Notes: Today marks the 13th anniversary when the legendary metal pioneers announced their reunion with original lineup members Ozzy Osbourne, Tony Iommi, and Geezer Butler for their final album "13." The announcement came during a press conference in their hometown of Birmingham, England, marking the end of years of speculation about a proper reunion. This momentous declaration would lead to their farewell tour and cement their legacy as the godfathers of heavy metal.

Today marks the 51st anniversary of this acclaimed fifth studio album that many consider their creative peak, reaching number 4 in the UK and showcasing the title track's innovative use of backwards vocals. The album demonstrated the band's songwriting maturity with tracks like "A National Acrobat" and "Spiral Architect" while maintaining their heavy metal foundation. Recorded at Rockfield Studios in Wales, the album proved Black Sabbath could balance accessibility with their signature darkness.

Born Blake Tollison Shelton in Ada, Oklahoma, he celebrates his 48th birthday today as one of country music's most recognizable stars and television personalities. His career includes over 40 chart singles with 28 number ones, plus his role as a coach on "The Voice" that brought him mainstream fame beyond country music. His blend of traditional country with pop sensibilities and his larger-than-life personality made him a crossover success.

Event: First single "I Wanna Get Better" released
Notes: Today marks the 10th anniversary when Jack Antonoff debuted his solo project with this uplifting anthem that reached number 1 on the Billboard Alternative chart. The track's themes of mental health recovery and personal growth resonated with listeners, establishing Bleachers as more than just a side project from his work with fun. The song's success launched Antonoff's parallel career as both a solo artist and one of pop music's most sought-after producers.

Born in Bergenfield, New Jersey, Jack Antonoff celebrates his 40th birthday today as one of modern pop's most influential producer-songwriters and the creative force behind Bleachers. His production work with Taylor Swift, Lorde, and Lana Del Rey has earned him multiple Grammy Awards, while his bands fun. and Bleachers showcase his own artistic vision. His ability to craft emotional, anthemic pop songs has made him a defining voice of 2010s indie and mainstream pop.

Today marks the 7th anniversary of this sophomore album that debuted at number 44 on the Billboard 200 and showcased Antonoff's evolution as both songwriter and producer. The record explored themes of loss and relationships through tracks like "Don't Take the Money" and "I Miss Those Days," featuring collaborations with Lorde and Carly Rae Jepsen. The album demonstrated greater sonic sophistication while maintaining the emotional directness that defines the Bleachers sound.

Today marks the 10th anniversary of this debut album that peaked at number 56 on the Billboard 200 and established Antonoff's solo vision beyond his work with fun. The record featured the hit single "I Wanna Get Better" and showcased his ability to blend nostalgic 1980s influences with contemporary indie pop sensibilities. Recorded while processing personal struggles and career transitions, the album's title perfectly captured its exploration of longing and emotional complexity.

Today marks the 9th anniversary of this covers album where various artists reimagined classic tracks, showcasing Antonoff's curatorial skills and collaborative spirit. The project featured artists like Grimes and Tinashe putting fresh spins on songs ranging from pop classics to indie deep cuts. This experimental release demonstrated Bleachers' role as more than just a recording project, serving as a creative hub for genre-blending collaborations.

Today marks the 29th anniversary of this ambitious second album that peaked at number 28 on the Billboard 200 but received mixed reviews compared to their breakthrough debut. The record showed the band experimenting with longer, more complex compositions while maintaining their signature psychedelic alternative rock sound. Released just months before Shannon Hoon's tragic death, the album represents the band's final statement with their original lineup and showcases their unrealized potential.

Born in Fontana, California, Travis Landon Barker celebrates his 49th birthday today as one of punk rock's most influential drummers and a multimedia entrepreneur. His explosive drumming style helped define Blink-182's sound on classics like "Enema of the State," while his collaborations across genres from hip-hop to electronic music showcase his versatility. His survival of a 2008 plane crash and subsequent recovery made him an inspiration beyond music.

Born Mark Allan Hoppus in Ridgecrest, California, he celebrates his 52nd birthday today as the co-founder, bassist, and primary songwriter of one of pop-punk's most successful bands. His melodic bass lines and distinctive vocals helped define tracks like "All the Small Things" and "What's My Age Again?," while his recent battle with cancer showed his resilience. His role in shaping late '90s pop-punk culture extends beyond music into fashion and attitude.

Event: Enema of the State album released
Notes: Today marks the 25th anniversary of this breakthrough third album that reached number 9 on the Billboard 200 and spawned massive hits like "What's My Age Again?" and "All the Small Things." The record perfected their blend of punk energy with pop melodies, making them MTV darlings and defining the sound of late '90s pop-punk. Producer Jerry Finn's polished production helped transform the trio from underground favorites into mainstream stars.

Event: Take Off Your Pants and Jacket album released
Notes: Today marks the 23rd anniversary of this chart-topping fourth album that debuted at number 1 and featured hits like "The Rock Show" and "First Date." The record represented the peak of their commercial success, selling over 14 million copies worldwide and cementing their status as pop-punk royalty. The album's blend of humor, relationships, and catchy hooks captured the essence of early 2000s youth culture.

Today marks the 27th anniversary of this major label debut that reached number 67 on the Billboard 200 and featured their breakthrough single "Dammit." The album showcased their evolution from skate punk roots toward the more melodic pop-punk sound that would make them superstars. Producer Mark Trombino helped refine their approach while maintaining the raw energy that connected with their growing fanbase.

Today marks the 8th anniversary of this comeback album that debuted at number 1 and featured the first new material with the classic lineup in over a decade, albeit with Matt Skiba replacing Tom DeLonge. The record proved the band could still craft infectious pop-punk anthems with tracks like "Bored to Death" while addressing more mature themes. The album's success demonstrated their enduring appeal and ability to evolve their sound for a new generation.

Today marks the 5th anniversary of this eighth studio album that reached number 3 on the Billboard 200 and continued their collaboration with Matt Skiba following Tom DeLonge's departure. The record featured a heavier, more experimental sound with tracks like "Blame It on My Youth" and "Darkside" showcasing electronic influences. The album demonstrated their willingness to push beyond traditional pop-punk boundaries while maintaining their core identity.

Today marks the 13th anniversary of this reunion album that debuted at number 2 and marked the return of Tom DeLonge after a four-year hiatus. The record featured a darker, more mature sound with tracks like "Up All Night" and "After Midnight" reflecting the band members' growth and experiences. Despite mixed critical reception, the album proved their commercial viability and set the stage for future creative directions.

Marks the return of the classic lineup with Tom DeLonge rejoining Mark Hoppus and Travis Barker after nearly a decade apart. The album peaked at #2 on the Billboard 200 and represents a mature evolution of their pop-punk sound while maintaining the energy that made them superstars in the late '90s and early 2000s.

Event: The Mark Tom & Travis Show: The Enema Strikes Back album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of this energetic live album that captured the band at their peak commercial popularity. Recorded during their Enema of the State tour, the album showcased their tight live performance and irreverent stage banter that endeared them to millions of fans worldwide.

Marks the release of their most experimental and mature work, featuring a darker, more introspective sound than previous efforts. The self-titled album reached #3 on the Billboard 200 and included hits like 'I Miss You' and 'Feeling This,' showing the band's evolution beyond simple pop-punk into more complex musical territory.

Born Thomas Matthew DeLonge Jr., he co-founded Blink-182 and helped define the pop-punk genre of the late '90s with his distinctive guitar tone and songwriting. Beyond music, DeLonge has pursued interests in UFO research and aerospace technology, founding To The Stars Academy and continuing to push creative boundaries.

Represents one of the earliest recordings from the pop-punk pioneers, originally released as a demo recording that showcased their raw energy and humor. This lo-fi collection of tracks helped establish their irreverent style and would later be reissued as their fan base grew exponentially throughout the decade.

Actually marks the single release rather than an album, as 'Call Me' was recorded for the American Gigolo soundtrack. The Giorgio Moroder-produced track became one of their biggest hits and demonstrated the band's ability to blend new wave with disco influences.

Event: Call Me goes #1 on US singles charts
Notes: Celebrates the peak chart success of this disco-influenced new wave anthem that dominated radio for six consecutive weeks. Written by Giorgio Moroder and Debbie Harry for the Richard Gere film American Gigolo, the song became the band's most successful single and epitomized the sound of 1980.

Event: Debbie Harry appears live as Blondie for the first time at CBGBs
Notes: The name is derived from comments made by truck drivers who catcalled "Hey, Blondie" to Harry as they drove by.

Event: Child Is Father To The Man album released
Notes: Marks the debut of this jazz-rock fusion group that emerged from the remains of The Blues Project. The album introduced their sophisticated blend of rock, jazz, blues, and classical music, featuring complex arrangements and the distinctive vocals that would define their sound throughout their most successful period.

Born in Brooklyn, Katz became a founding member and guitarist for this pioneering jazz-rock fusion band after leaving The Blues Project. His guitar work helped define the group's sophisticated sound on classics like 'Child Is Father to the Man' and their Grammy-winning second album.

Celebrates the debut from this Long Island hard rock band that would become cult legends. Produced by Sandy Pearlman, the album introduced their mysterious mythology and heavy, riff-driven sound that would later spawn classics like 'Don't Fear the Reaper' and establish them as pioneers of arena rock.

Born Daniel Edward Aykroyd, he co-created the Blues Brothers concept with John Belushi on Saturday Night Live, transforming it into a legitimate musical act. The comedian and actor's passion for blues music led to successful albums and the hit 1980 film, introducing classic R&B to a new generation.

Marks the debut album from this jam band that emerged from Princeton University and became central to the 1990s jam scene. Led by harmonica virtuoso John Popper, the album established their improvisational style and blues-rock foundation that would lead to massive success with 'Four' just a few years later.

Born Ellas Otha Bates, he revolutionized rock and roll with his distinctive rectangular guitar and the syncopated rhythm pattern that bears his name. His innovative 'Bo Diddley beat' influenced countless musicians from Buddy Holly to the Rolling Stones and helped establish the fundamental rhythmic backbone of rock music.

Event: The Times They Are A-Changin' album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of this prophetic collection that cemented Dylan's role as the voice of social change in 1960s America. Featuring the iconic title track and 'With God on Our Side,' the album captured the spirit of the civil rights movement and established protest songs as a vital part of the folk revival.

Event: Blood On The Tracks album released
Notes: Marks the release of what many consider Dylan's masterpiece, recorded during a period of personal turmoil and marital difficulties. The album reached #1 and featured classics like 'Tangled Up in Blue' and 'Simple Twist of Fate,' showcasing his return to acoustic-based music with deeply personal and poetic lyrics.
Debut album

Celebrates Dylan's successful venture into country music, recorded in Nashville with session legends like Charlie Daniels and featuring his newly mellowed voice. The album reached #3 and included the hit duet 'Girl from the North Country' with Johnny Cash, marking a surprising but successful stylistic departure.

Marks the release of this groundbreaking double album that many consider the first great rock double LP. Recorded in Nashville and New York, it featured the epic 'Sad Eyed Lady of the Lowlands' and showcased Dylan's surreal, stream-of-consciousness songwriting at its most adventurous and influential.

Event: Together Through Life goes #1 on US album charts
Notes: Celebrates Dylan achieving his first #1 album in nearly 30 years, proving his enduring relevance in his late career. The collaboration-heavy album featured duets and showcased his continued ability to reinvent his sound while maintaining the poetic prowess that made him a cultural icon.

Born Robert Allen Zimmerman in Duluth, Minnesota, this marks the birthday of arguably the most influential songwriter of the 20th century. His groundbreaking shift from acoustic folk to electric rock at the 1965 Newport Folk Festival fundamentally changed popular music, while his poetic, socially conscious lyrics earned him the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2016. From protest anthems like "Blowin' in the Wind" to rock classics like "Like a Rolling Stone," he transformed what popular song could be.

Event: The Freewheelin’ Bob Dylan album released
Notes: This landmark second album established Dylan as the voice of a generation and cemented folk music's role in the protest movement. Featuring classics "Blowin' in the Wind" and "A Hard Rain's A-Gonna Fall," the album reached number 22 on the Billboard 200 and became a soundtrack for the civil rights movement. The album's blend of traditional folk melodies with contemporary social commentary created a template that countless artists would follow.

Event: Pat Garrett & Billy The Kid album released
Notes: This soundtrack album for Sam Peckinpah's Western film marked Dylan's first major film score work and featured the hit single "Knockin' on Heaven's Door." While the album itself received mixed reviews and modest commercial success, "Knockin' on Heaven's Door" became one of his most covered songs and a classic rock staple. The project represented Dylan's continued exploration of American musical traditions during his early 1970s period.

Born Michael Bernard Bloomfield in Chicago, this marks the birthday of the legendary blues guitarist who played a pivotal role in Dylan's electric transformation. He was the lead guitarist on Dylan's groundbreaking "Highway 61 Revisited" album and famously backed him during the controversial electric performance at the 1965 Newport Folk Festival. His work with the Paul Butterfield Blues Band and Electric Flag helped bridge blues and rock, influencing countless guitarists including Eric Clapton and Carlos Santana.

Born Charles Wayne Sexton in San Antonio, Texas, this marks the birthday of the guitarist who became a key member of Dylan's touring band in the late 1990s and 2000s. He first gained attention as a teenage guitar prodigy with his 1985 hit "Beat's So Lonely" before establishing himself as a respected session musician. His work with Dylan, Arc Angels, and as a solo artist showcased his versatility across rock, blues, and Americana genres.

Event: The Bootleg Series, Vol. 10 album released
Notes: Subtitled "Another Self Portrait (1969-1971)," this installment of Dylan's archival series revisited one of his most controversial periods with previously unreleased material. The collection helped rehabilitate the reputation of the original "Self Portrait" album by revealing alternate versions and outtakes that showed Dylan's exploration of American roots music. Critics praised how these recordings provided new context for understanding Dylan's artistic choices during this transitional period.

This album marked Dylan's third consecutive number one debut, proving his enduring commercial relevance at age 65. Part of his late-career renaissance that began with "Time Out of Mind," the album featured originals like "Thunder on the Mountain" alongside his interpretation of traditional songs. Critics praised Dylan's gravelly vocals and the album's blend of blues, rockabilly, and folk, with many considering it among his strongest work in decades.

This seminal album completed Dylan's transition from folk troubadour to rock innovator and peaked at number three on the Billboard 200. The opening track "Like a Rolling Stone" became his biggest hit, reaching number two on the singles chart and revolutionizing popular music with its six-minute length and surreal lyrics. Recorded with members of the Paul Butterfield Blues Band, including Mike Bloomfield, the album's electric sound alienated some folk purists but attracted a massive new audience.
Guest performers include Neil Young, Eric Clapton, George Harrison, Roger McGuinn, Tom Petty, Ronnie Wood and Dylan himself.

Event: The Bootleg Series Vol. 12 album released
Notes: Subtitled "The Cutting Edge 1965-1966," this extensive collection documents Dylan's pivotal transition to electric rock music across multiple formats. The series revealed alternate takes and previously unreleased material from the sessions that produced "Bringing It All Back Home," "Highway 61 Revisited," and "Blonde on Blonde." The deluxe edition's six CDs provided unprecedented insight into Dylan's creative process during what many consider his most innovative period.
Jah

This album showcased Marley's more mellow, spiritually focused side with tracks like "Is This Love" and "Satisfy My Soul" becoming international hits. Recorded during his self-imposed exile in London, the album reflected his Rastafarian beliefs while maintaining the accessible sound that brought reggae to worldwide audiences. The title track celebrated cannabis as a sacrament, while the overall album demonstrated reggae's versatility beyond protest music.

Born Neville O'Riley Livingston in Kingston, Jamaica, this marks the birthday of the founding member of The Wailers alongside Bob Marley and Peter Tosh. As the group's percussionist and harmony vocalist, he helped create the classic Wailers sound on early albums like "Catch a Fire" and "Burnin'." After leaving the group in 1973 to focus on his Rastafarian faith, he pursued a successful solo career, winning three Grammy Awards and becoming known as reggae's elder statesman.

This breakthrough album introduced reggae to international rock audiences and marked The Wailers' major label debut on Island Records. Producer Chris Blackwell added rock guitar and keyboard overdubs to make the music more accessible to Western ears, while preserving the group's revolutionary message on tracks like "Stir It Up" and "Concrete Jungle." The album's success paved the way for reggae's global expansion and established Marley as music's leading Third World voice.

This album became Marley's biggest commercial success in America, reaching number eight on the Billboard 200 and marking reggae's highest chart position at the time. Featuring the hit "Roots, Rock, Reggae" and the militant "War," which quoted a Haile Selassie speech, the album balanced accessibility with political consciousness. The success demonstrated that American audiences were ready for reggae's message of social justice and Rastafarian spirituality.

Widely considered Marley's masterpiece, this album was recorded during his London exile following a 1976 assassination attempt in Jamaica. The title track and "Three Little Birds" became international anthems, while songs like "Jamming" and "One Love" spread reggae's message of unity worldwide. Time magazine later named it the best album of the 20th century, cementing its status as reggae's most influential recording.

This final studio album before Marley's death showcased his spiritual maturity and featured some of his most enduring songs, including "Could You Be Loved" and "Redemption Song." The acoustic "Redemption Song," with its references to Marcus Garvey, became his most personal statement about mental freedom and emancipation. The album reached the top 10 in multiple countries and demonstrated Marley's evolution from revolutionary to spiritual teacher.

Event: Songs Of Freedom box set released
Notes: This comprehensive four-CD box set compiled Marley's greatest work alongside rare tracks and previously unreleased material, becoming the definitive Bob Marley collection. Released eleven years after his death, it introduced his music to a new generation while providing longtime fans with alternate versions and deeper cuts. The set's commercial success proved Marley's enduring influence and helped establish the template for other reggae reissue projects.

This album marked Marley's first recording with the I-Threes (including his wife Rita) and featured some of his most politically charged material. The title track celebrated African identity while "No Woman No Cry" became one of his most beloved songs, showcasing his ability to blend personal reflection with universal themes. Recorded after Peter Tosh and Bunny Wailer left The Wailers, it established the classic Bob Marley and the Wailers lineup that would define his later career.

This live album captured the energy of Marley's 1978 European tour and demonstrated his power as a performer at the height of his international fame. Recorded primarily at the Pavillon de Paris, the album featured extended versions of classics like "Positive Vibration" and "Exodus" that showcased the band's tight interplay. The recording proved that reggae could fill arenas worldwide and documented Marley's role as music's most charismatic Third World ambassador.

The legendary drummer was born Carlton Lloyd Barrett and became the rhythmic backbone of Bob Marley and the Wailers. His innovative drumming style helped define the reggae sound on classic albums like Catch a Fire and Exodus. Barrett's precise, understated playing provided the foundation for some of reggae's most influential recordings until his tragic death in 1987.

Born Robert Arthur Mould, he first gained fame as the guitarist and primary songwriter for the influential hardcore punk band Hüsker Dü in the 1980s. After the band's dissolution, he found success with the alternative rock power trio Sugar and later as a solo artist. Mould is celebrated for his ability to blend melody with aggressive guitar work and his honest, introspective songwriting.

Born Robert Clark Seger in Detroit, he became one of rock's most enduring heartland voices with his raspy vocals and working-class anthems. His breakthrough came with the album Night Moves in 1976, followed by a string of hits throughout the late 1970s and 1980s. Seger's music perfectly captured the spirit of Middle America with songs like 'Old Time Rock and Roll' and 'Against the Wind.'

This album marked the peak of Seger's commercial success, reaching number one on the Billboard 200 and earning him his first Grammy Award. The title track became one of his signature songs, while 'Fire Lake' also became a radio staple. The album showcased Seger's mature songwriting and his ability to blend rock with folk influences, cementing his status as a heartland rock icon.

Born Robert Clark Seger in Detroit, he became one of rock's most enduring heartland voices with his raspy vocals and working-class anthems. His breakthrough came with the album Night Moves in 1976, followed by a string of hits throughout the late 1970s and 1980s. Seger's music perfectly captured the spirit of Middle America with songs like 'Old Time Rock and Roll' and 'Against the Wind.'

This breakthrough album launched Seger into national stardom after years of regional success in the Midwest. The title track and 'Mainstreet' became classic rock staples, showcasing his storytelling ability and nostalgic themes. Recorded with his Silver Bullet Band, the album reached number eight on the charts and established Seger as a major force in American rock music.

Born Robert Barisford Brown, he first gained fame as a member of the R&B group New Edition before launching a successful solo career. His 1988 album Don't Be Cruel produced multiple hit singles including 'My Prerogative' and helped define the new jack swing sound. Brown became known for his energetic performances and later for his tumultuous marriage to Whitney Houston.

Born Robert Keith McFerrin Jr., he revolutionized vocal music with his extraordinary range and innovative use of his voice as a complete musical instrument. His 1988 a cappella hit 'Don't Worry Be Happy' became a global phenomenon and topped the Billboard Hot 100. McFerrin is renowned for his ability to create complex harmonies and rhythms using only his voice, earning him multiple Grammy Awards.

Ice-T's metal band created one of the most controversial albums in music history, sparking nationwide debate over the track 'Cop Killer.' The album fused hardcore punk and heavy metal with Ice-T's street-wise rap lyrics, pioneering the rap-metal genre. Despite being pulled from stores due to pressure from law enforcement groups and politicians, the album became a landmark in discussions about censorship and artistic freedom.

Event: Bon Iver, Bon Iver album released
Notes: Justin Vernon's second album under the Bon Iver moniker marked a dramatic evolution from his lo-fi debut For Emma, Forever Ago. The self-titled release featured lush orchestrations and complex arrangements that earned critical acclaim and a Grammy Award for Best Alternative Album. Songs like 'Holocene' showcased Vernon's distinctive falsetto and his ability to create deeply atmospheric indie folk music.

The New Jersey band's debut album introduced their anthemic hard rock sound to the world, though it achieved only modest commercial success initially. Tracks like 'Runaway' and 'She Don't Know Me' established Jon Bon Jovi's knack for crafting memorable hooks and relatable lyrics. The album laid the groundwork for what would become one of the most successful rock bands of the 1980s.

Born John Francis Bongiovi Jr., he formed Bon Jovi in 1983 and became one of rock's most recognizable frontmen. The band achieved massive success with albums like Slippery When Wet and New Jersey, producing anthems like 'Livin' on a Prayer' and 'You Give Love a Bad Name.' Beyond music, he has pursued acting and philanthropy, maintaining his status as a rock icon for over four decades.
Jon Bon Jovi, Richie Sambora and Alec John Such formed Bon Jovi

Released during the grunge era, this album showed Bon Jovi adapting to the changing musical landscape with a more mature, alternative rock-influenced sound. The album spawned hits like 'This Ain't a Love Song' and 'Something for the Pain,' reaching the top 10 in multiple countries. Despite mixed critical reception, it demonstrated the band's ability to evolve while maintaining their core fanbase.

Born Richard Stephen Sambora, he became Bon Jovi's lead guitarist and primary songwriting partner, co-writing many of their biggest hits. His guitar work on classics like 'Livin' on a Prayer' and 'Wanted Dead or Alive' helped define the band's arena rock sound. Sambora also pursued a successful solo career and was known for his distinctive playing style that blended hard rock with blues influences.

Jon Bon Jovi's solo album served as the soundtrack to the Western film Young Guns II, featuring contributions from Elton John, Jeff Beck, and Little Richard. The title track became a number one hit, proving Bon Jovi's appeal extended beyond the band context. The album successfully bridged his rock sensibilities with a more cinematic, Americana-influenced sound.

This album catapulted Bon Jovi to superstardom, spending eight weeks at number one and selling over 28 million copies worldwide. Anthems like 'Livin' on a Prayer,' 'You Give Love a Bad Name,' and 'Wanted Dead or Alive' became defining songs of 1980s rock. The album's polished production and universally relatable themes about love and perseverance made it a cultural phenomenon.

The follow-up to Slippery When Wet continued the band's commercial dominance, debuting at number one and producing five top 40 hits. Songs like 'Bad Medicine' and 'I'll Be There for You' maintained their arena rock formula while exploring slightly more personal themes. The album's success cemented Bon Jovi's status as one of the premier rock bands of the late 1980s.

Event: Have a Nice Day album released
Notes: This comeback album marked Bon Jovi's return to their harder rock roots after experimenting with different sounds in the late 1990s. The title track became a radio hit and helped re-establish the band's presence in the rock mainstream during the mid-2000s. The album debuted at number two on the Billboard 200, proving the band's enduring commercial appeal after more than two decades.

Born Hector Juan Samuel Torres, he joined Bon Jovi as drummer in 1983 and became known for his steady, powerful playing style. His rhythmic foundation helped drive classics like 'Livin' on a Prayer' and 'You Give Love a Bad Name' throughout the band's most successful period. Torres also pursued interests in art and jewelry design alongside his musical career with one of rock's most enduring bands.

Event: New Jersey starts a 4-week run at #1
Notes: The album produced five Billboard Hot 100 Top 10 singles, the most top ten hits to date for a hard rock album.

Event: Slippery When Wet hits #1 on US album charts
Notes: The album went on to sell over 8 million copies world wide.

Marks the anniversary of the band's fifth studio album, which reached number 5 on the Billboard 200 and marked their transition to a more mature, introspective sound. The album spawned hits like the title track and "Bed of Roses," helping the band maintain relevance in the grunge era. It was their first album without founding member Alec John Such and demonstrated their ability to evolve beyond their 1980s arena rock roots.

Celebrates the Cleveland rap group's breakthrough album that peaked at number 1 on the Billboard 200 and established their signature rapid-fire, melodic flow style. The album featured the massive hit "Tha Crossroads," which became a number 1 single and won a Grammy Award. Their innovative harmonized rap approach influenced countless hip-hop artists and helped bring Midwest rap to mainstream prominence.

Event: Performs "I'm Afraid Of Americans" on Howard Stern Tribute to David Bowie
Notes: Commemorates a tribute performance honoring the late David Bowie on Howard Stern's radio show. The performance highlighted Bowie's lasting influence on contemporary artists nearly two years after his passing in 2016. The tribute demonstrated how Bowie's experimental catalog continues to inspire new interpretations across different musical generations.

Marks the debut festival performance at Alabama's premier music festival on the Gulf Coast. The appearance represented an important milestone for emerging artists breaking into the major festival circuit. Hangout Festival has become a significant platform for both established and developing acts since its inception in 2010.

Event: Beastie Boys Final Performance with Adam Yauch
Notes: Commemorates the final live performance featuring all three original members of the pioneering hip-hop trio. This Tennessee festival appearance became historically significant following Adam Yauch's cancer diagnosis and subsequent death in 2012. The performance captured the end of an era for one of rap's most influential and innovative groups.

Event: Bobby Womack Performance - Final performance before passing
Notes: Marks the legendary soul singer's final performance before his death just weeks later on June 27, 2014. The appearance showcased the R&B icon's enduring artistry and connection with audiences even in his final years. His Bonnaroo set served as a poignant farewell for the "Midnight Mover" singer who influenced generations of soul and rock artists.

Celebrates the inaugural year of what would become one of America's premier music festivals in Manchester, Tennessee. The first festival featured headliners Trey Anastasio, Widespread Panic, and other jam band favorites, establishing the event's initial focus on improvisational music. This marked the beginning of Bonnaroo's evolution into a major multi-genre festival destination attracting over 80,000 annual attendees.

Celebrates the birthday of Bonnie Lynn Raitt, daughter of Broadway star John Raitt, who became one of blues and rock's most respected slide guitarists. Her career breakthrough came with 1989's "Nick of Time," which won three Grammy Awards and revitalized her career after years of critical acclaim but modest commercial success. She's won 10 Grammy Awards total and was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2000 for her masterful blend of blues, rock, and R&B.

Marks the birthday of Gaynor Hopkins, the Welsh singer who became famous for her powerful, raspy voice on anthemic rock ballads. She achieved international stardom with "Total Eclipse of the Heart" in 1983, which topped charts worldwide and became one of the best-selling singles of all time. Her dramatic vocal style and theatrical performances made her a defining voice of 1980s power ballads.

Celebrates the birthday of Booker Taliaferro Jones Jr., the legendary keyboardist and bandleader who helped define the Memphis soul sound. He wrote and performed the instrumental classic "Green Onions" at age 17, which became a cornerstone of 1960s soul music. As the leader of Booker T. & The M.G.'s, he served as the house band for Stax Records, backing artists like Otis Redding, Sam & Dave, and Wilson Pickett.

Marks the birthday of Steven Lee Cropper, the influential guitarist whose crisp, economical playing style became a hallmark of Memphis soul music. As a member of Booker T. & The M.G.'s and a key songwriter at Stax Records, he co-wrote classics like "(Sittin' On) The Dock of the Bay" with Otis Redding and "In the Midnight Hour" with Wilson Pickett. His guitar work influenced generations of musicians and helped create the distinctive Stax Records sound.
Bass funk legend has played with James Brown, Parliament, Funkadelic, and Bootsy's Rubber Band

Celebrates the birthday of Donald Thomas Scholz, the MIT-trained engineer who revolutionized rock music through his innovative use of technology and home recording. He spent years perfecting Boston's debut album in his basement studio, creating a signature sound through custom-built equipment and meticulous layering. His perfectionist approach and technical innovations influenced both recording techniques and the development of guitar effects pedals.

Marks the release of the band's third studio album, which reached number 7 on the Billboard 200 despite being their first album in eight years. The extended gap between releases reflected Tom Scholz's perfectionist approach to recording and legal battles with the band's record label. While not matching their earlier commercial peaks, the album maintained their signature layered guitar sound and melodic sensibilities.

Commemorates the highly anticipated follow-up to one of rock's most successful debut albums, which reached number 1 on the Billboard 200. Despite enormous commercial expectations, the album faced the impossible task of matching their debut's unprecedented success, though it still sold over 7 million copies. The title track and "A Man I'll Never Be" became radio staples, but the two-year gap between albums created pressure that would plague the band throughout their career.

Celebrates one of rock history's most successful debut albums, which spent 17 weeks at number 3 on the Billboard 200 and sold over 25 million copies worldwide. Tom Scholz recorded most of the album in his basement studio using custom-built equipment, creating a revolutionary layered guitar sound that influenced arena rock for decades. Hits like "More Than a Feeling" and "Peace of Mind" established Boston as masters of melodic hard rock and set an impossibly high standard for their future releases.

Marks the birthday of Shad Gregory Moss, who became one of hip-hop's most successful child rappers under the mentorship of Snoop Dogg. He achieved mainstream success with hits like "Bounce with Me" and later transitioned into acting with roles in films and television. His career trajectory from child prodigy to adult entertainer demonstrated the challenges and opportunities facing young artists in hip-hop.

Celebrates the birthday of George Alan O'Dowd, the flamboyant frontman of Culture Club who became one of the most recognizable figures of 1980s new wave. Culture Club achieved massive success with hits like "Do You Really Want to Hurt Me" and "Karma Chameleon," with his androgynous appearance and soulful voice challenging gender norms in popular music. His bold fashion choices and theatrical persona made him a cultural icon who helped define the colorful, boundary-pushing spirit of early MTV.

Born Nathan Alexander Morris, he celebrates his birthday as one of the founding members and tenor voices of the legendary R&B group Boyz II Men. His harmonies helped create timeless classics like "End of the Road" and "I'll Make Love to You," making Boyz II Men one of the best-selling R&B groups of all time. The group's distinctive sound and Morris's vocal contributions defined 1990s soul music.

Born William Royce Scaggs, he celebrates his birthday as a master of blue-eyed soul and sophisticated pop-rock. His 1976 album "Silk Degrees" spawned hits like "Lido Shuffle" and "Lowdown," establishing him as a smooth-voiced crooner with jazz and R&B sensibilities. His career spans from early collaborations with Steve Miller to his successful solo ventures that bridged rock and adult contemporary audiences.

Event: By The Way, I Forgive You album released
Notes: This album marked Carlile's commercial and critical breakthrough, earning her three Grammy Awards including Best Americana Album. The deeply personal record explored themes of forgiveness, motherhood, and self-acceptance through powerful tracks like "The Joke" and "Hold Out Your Hand." The album's success solidified her position as one of contemporary Americana's most important voices.

This album represented a creative peak for Carlile, showcasing her evolution as both a songwriter and vocalist. Produced by Trina Shoemaker, the record featured standout tracks like "The Eye" and "Mainstream Kid" that demonstrated her ability to blend folk, rock, and country influences. The album earned critical acclaim and helped expand her fanbase beyond the folk music community.

This breakthrough album introduced Carlile to mainstream audiences and established her as a formidable singer-songwriter. The title track became her signature song, showcasing her powerful vocals and honest storytelling that would define her career. Produced by T Bone Burnett, the album marked her transition from indie folk artist to nationally recognized performer.

Event: Broken Horses: A Memoir book released
Notes: Her memoir marks the birthday celebration with a deeply personal account of her journey from small-town Washington to Grammy-winning artist. The book chronicles her struggles with identity, sexuality, and finding her voice in the music industry while maintaining her authenticity. "Broken Horses" became a New York Times bestseller and revealed the stories behind many of her most beloved songs.

Born in Ravensdale, Washington, she celebrates her birthday as one of contemporary Americana's most powerful voices and advocates. Her career spans from indie folk beginnings to Grammy-winning mainstream success, with her 2018 album "By The Way, I Forgive You" earning three Grammy Awards. She's known for her soaring vocals, honest songwriting, and activism for LGBTQ+ rights and social justice causes.

This album marked Carlile's fourth studio effort, recorded at Bear Creek Studio in Woodinville, Washington. The record featured a more rootsy, Americana sound with tracks like "That Wasn't Me" and "A Promise to Keep" showcasing her continued growth as a songwriter. The album demonstrated her ability to balance intimate acoustic moments with full-band arrangements.

Her self-titled debut album introduced the music world to Carlile's distinctive voice and songwriting talent. Recorded when she was just 23, the album featured early versions of songs that would become fan favorites and established the foundation for her folk-rock sound. The record showcased her ability to convey deep emotion through both powerful vocals and stripped-down acoustic arrangements.

This supergroup album brought together Carlile with Natalie Hemby, Maren Morris, and Amanda Shires to create a powerful statement about women in country music. The self-titled debut featured the hit single "Redesigning Women" and addressed themes of female empowerment and social justice. The project earned critical acclaim and a Grammy Award for Best Country Song.

Event: In The Canyon Haze (ITSD Deluxe Edition) album released
Notes: This deluxe edition expanded her acclaimed "In These Silent Days" album with additional tracks and alternate versions. The release celebrated the success of the original album while providing fans with deeper insight into the creative process. The expanded collection included live recordings and demos that showcased different facets of the songs.

Event: In These Silent Days album released
Notes: This album continued Carlile's hot streak following her Grammy success, debuting at number 11 on the Billboard 200. The record featured introspective tracks like "Right on Time" and "Broken Horses" that explored themes of family, aging, and personal growth. Recorded during the pandemic, the album reflected the contemplative mood of the times while maintaining her signature emotional intensity.

Event: Give Up The Ghost album released
Notes: This album marked Carlile's third studio release and showcased her growing confidence as both a performer and songwriter. The record featured a fuller sound with tracks like "Dreams" and "Before It Breaks" that demonstrated her ability to craft both intimate ballads and anthemic rockers. The album helped bridge the gap between her early folk work and her later mainstream breakthrough.

Event: Brandon and Leah announce their pregnancy
Notes: The musical duo shared the exciting news of expecting their first child together, marking a new chapter in both their personal lives and creative partnership. The announcement came as they were building momentum with their folk-pop sound and family connections to the entertainment industry. Their growing family would later influence their songwriting and artistic direction.

This EP showcased the duo's harmony-driven folk-pop sound and featured collaborations that highlighted their musical chemistry. The release helped establish their artistic identity separate from their family connections and demonstrated their songwriting abilities. "Cronies" received positive reception from folk and indie music communities.

The couple's wedding marked the official beginning of their personal and professional partnership in music. Their marriage brought together two artists who would go on to create music that explored themes of love, family, and relationships. The union was celebrated within the entertainment community given Brandon's family connections.

Born Brandon Thompson Jenner, he celebrates his birthday as a singer-songwriter and member of the duo Brandon and Leah. The son of Caitlyn Jenner and Linda Thompson, he carved out his own musical path in folk-pop and indie music.
