Notes: Today marks the release of Sign o' the Times, widely considered Prince's creative masterpiece and one of the greatest albums of the 1980s. Originally conceived as a triple album, the final double-LP showcased Prince's incredible range across funk, rock, soul, and pop, featuring classics like 'If I Was Your Girlfriend' and the apocalyptic title track. The album demonstrated Prince at his most socially conscious while maintaining his musical innovation and sexual frankness.

This date celebrates the release of Parade, the vibrant soundtrack to Prince's film Under the Cherry Moon. The album spawned the chart-topping 'Kiss,' one of Prince's most minimalist and effective singles, built around a sparse drum machine and guitar riff. Parade blended psychedelic pop with French chanson influences, reflecting the film's Côte d'Azur setting and Prince's continued musical evolution following Purple Rain's massive success.

Today marks the release of For You, Prince's remarkable debut album recorded when he was just 19 years old. Prince played every instrument and sang every vocal part, establishing the multi-instrumental prowess that would define his career. While commercially modest, the album introduced Prince's falsetto vocals and sophisticated production techniques, laying the groundwork for his revolutionary approach to music-making.

Event: Kiss goes #1 on US singles charts
Notes: Prince also had the No.2 song 'Manic Monday', by The Bangles, which he wrote under the pseudonym 'Christopher.'

This date celebrates the release of Musicology, Prince's triumphant return to mainstream success and critical acclaim. The album earned him two Grammy Awards and marked his first Top 5 Billboard album in years, driven by a innovative touring strategy that included the CD with concert tickets. The title track and 'Call My Name' showcased Prince's ability to blend contemporary R&B with his classic Minneapolis sound.

Today marks the passing of Prince Rogers Nelson at age 57 at his Paisley Park estate in Minnesota. One of music's greatest innovators and most prolific artists, Prince revolutionized popular music through his genre-blending artistry, virtuosic musicianship, and uncompromising creative vision. His influence spans multiple generations of artists, and his catalog of classics from 'Purple Rain' to 'Sign o' the Times' ensures his legacy as one of the most important musicians of all time.

Event: Around the World in a Day album released
Notes: This date celebrates the release of Around the World in a Day, Prince's bold artistic statement following Purple Rain's massive commercial success. The psychedelic-influenced album deliberately moved away from the rock sound that made him a superstar, incorporating Middle Eastern instruments and paisley-soaked mysticism. Despite confusing some fans initially, the album spawned hits like 'Raspberry Beret' and demonstrated Prince's refusal to repeat himself artistically.

Event: Rave In2 The Joy Fantastic album released
Notes: Today marks the release of Rave In2 the Joy Fantastic, a collaborative album that featured guest appearances from Chuck D, Gwen Stefani, and Sheryl Crow. The album represented Prince's attempt to engage with contemporary pop and hip-hop trends while maintaining his distinctive sound. Though not a commercial breakthrough, tracks like 'The Greatest Romance Ever Sold' showed Prince's continued ability to craft memorable pop hooks.

This date celebrates the release of Lovesexy, Prince's spiritual and sensual opus that served as his response to the dark themes of the unreleased Black Album. Conceived as a single continuous piece of music, the album blended funk, rock, and gospel into a cohesive statement about love conquering negativity. The controversial cover art and singles like 'Alphabet St.' reinforced Prince's reputation as music's most unpredictable major artist.

Today marks the release of One Nite Alone..., an intimate solo piano album that showcased Prince's skills as a composer and interpreter. Recorded primarily on acoustic piano, the album included both new compositions and reimagined versions of previous songs, offering fans a rare glimpse of Prince in his most vulnerable artistic mode. The stripped-down approach highlighted his often-overlooked abilities as a traditional songwriter and pianist.

Event: Around The World In A Day goes #1 on US album charts
Notes: This date marks Around The World In A Day reaching number one on the US album charts, proving Prince's commercial power even when challenging his audience. Despite being a dramatic departure from Purple Rain's rock-oriented sound, the album's success demonstrated Prince's ability to lead rather than follow musical trends. The chart achievement validated Prince's artistic risk-taking and cemented his status as one of the few artists who could experiment while maintaining massive popularity.

Today celebrates the birth of Prince Rogers Nelson in Minneapolis, Minnesota, who would become one of music's most innovative and influential artists. Born to jazz musician John Nelson and singer Mattie Shaw, Prince showed early musical genius and would go on to sell over 100 million records worldwide. His groundbreaking fusion of rock, funk, pop, and R&B, combined with his virtuosic musicianship and uncompromising artistic vision, revolutionized popular music and influenced countless artists across multiple generations.

This date marks the release of Chaos and Disorder, Prince's final album for Warner Bros. before achieving his long-sought independence. The deliberately raw and unpolished album served as Prince's middle finger to his former label, featuring garage rock aesthetics and minimal production. Despite its confrontational nature, tracks like 'Dinner with Delores' showed Prince's continued ability to craft compelling songs even in his most rebellious moments.

Today celebrates the release of Batman, Prince's soundtrack to Tim Burton's dark superhero film that became one of his most successful albums. The album topped the Billboard 200 for six consecutive weeks and spawned the number-one hit 'Batdance,' along with the sultry 'Partyman.' Prince's ability to craft music that both served the film's narrative and worked as standalone pop songs demonstrated his versatility and commercial instincts at their peak.

The album that transformed Prince into a global superstar peaked at number 6 on the Billboard 200 and spent 24 consecutive weeks in the top 10. The soundtrack to his semi-autobiographical film featured iconic tracks like "When Doves Cry" and the title track, both of which topped the Billboard Hot 100. The album's blend of rock, pop, R&B, and new wave established Prince as one of the most innovative artists of the 1980s and remains his most commercially successful release.

This marks the anniversary of Prince's unique distribution strategy where he gave away the album exclusively through European newspapers before its official release. The album was distributed through the Daily Mirror in the UK and other publications across Europe, bypassing traditional record label channels. Though it received mixed reviews and limited commercial success, it demonstrated Prince's continued experimentation with music distribution models in the digital age.

The US release followed Prince's innovative European distribution through newspapers, marking another experiment in alternative album distribution. The album featured a return to guitar-heavy funk and rock, with tracks like "Guitar" showcasing his instrumental prowess. Despite lukewarm commercial reception, the release demonstrated Prince's willingness to challenge traditional music industry practices while maintaining his prolific output.

This instrumental jazz-fusion album marked a departure from Prince's typical vocal-driven work, consisting entirely of four extended jams named after compass directions. The album was recorded live in the studio with a four-piece band and showcased Prince's skills as a guitarist and bandleader. Though it received limited commercial attention, it demonstrated his versatility and willingness to explore different musical territories outside mainstream expectations.

Released during Prince's transition away from his birth name, this album peaked at number 15 on the Billboard 200 and featured the hit single "Letitgo." The album explored themes of spirituality and rebirth, reflecting Prince's personal transformation during this period. Though it received mixed critical reception, it marked an important phase in his artistic evolution as he prepared to adopt his famous symbol.

Serving as the soundtrack to Prince's second film, the album peaked at number 6 on the Billboard 200 and featured collaborations with various artists including Tevin Campbell and Mavis Staples. The title track and "Thieves in the Temple" became notable hits, though the album received mixed reviews compared to his previous soundtrack success with Purple Rain. The project marked Prince's continued interest in multimedia artistic expression through film and music.

Event: The Vault: Old Friends For Sale album released
Notes: This compilation album featured previously unreleased tracks and alternate versions from Prince's extensive vault of recordings. The release peaked at number 85 on the Billboard 200 and included the single "She Spoke 2 Me," offering fans a glimpse into his unreleased catalog. The album demonstrated Prince's vast creative output and his practice of continuously recording material beyond what appeared on his official releases.

This marks the first album in Prince's HITnRUN series, released exclusively through Tidal streaming service as part of his exploration of digital distribution. The album featured collaborations with 3rdEyeGirl and included tracks like "HARDROCKLOVER" that showcased his continued relevance in contemporary music. The release strategy reflected his ongoing commitment to controlling his artistic output and embracing new technology platforms.

The lead single from his breakthrough double album became one of Prince's most iconic songs, reaching number 12 on the Billboard Hot 100. The track's apocalyptic theme and infectious groove helped establish Prince as a major force in popular music, while its futuristic production showcased his studio innovation. The song's enduring popularity was highlighted when it experienced renewed interest as the actual year 1999 approached.

Released during Prince's Symbol era, this album peaked at number 6 on the Billboard 200 and featured hits like "The Most Beautiful Girl in the World" and "Gold." The album was notable for its cohesive artistic vision and marked his return to more commercially accessible material after experimental releases. Critics praised it as one of his strongest works of the 1990s, showcasing his continued ability to blend genres and create compelling musical narratives.

Released simultaneously with Plectrumelectrum, this album featured Prince performing solo and was distributed exclusively through Target stores. The album showcased a more intimate, piano-driven side of his artistry with tracks exploring themes of love and spirituality. Though it received limited commercial promotion, it demonstrated Prince's continued prolific output and his ability to create contrasting musical statements.

Released as a companion to Art Official Age, this album featured Prince with his band 3rdEyeGirl in a guitar-heavy rock format. The album peaked at number 19 on the Billboard 200 and showcased Prince's return to a more aggressive, rock-oriented sound. The simultaneous release of two contrasting albums demonstrated his artistic range and continued ability to work across multiple musical styles.

The album marked Prince's return to major commercial success, reaching number 3 on the Billboard 200 and spawning multiple hit singles including the title track and "Cream." Featuring his new backing band The New Power Generation, the album blended hip-hop influences with Prince's signature funk and pop sensibilities. The release demonstrated his ability to adapt to changing musical trends while maintaining his distinctive artistic identity.
Prince's third studio album, mostly recorded in his home studio with him playing all instruments.

Also known as the "Love Symbol Album," this release coincided with Prince's adoption of an unpronounceable symbol as his stage name. The album peaked at number 5 on the Billboard 200 and featured hits like "My Name Is Prince" and "7," showcasing his continued musical innovation during a period of industry rebellion. The release marked a pivotal moment in his ongoing battle for artistic control and ownership of his master recordings.

This album established Prince as a provocative and innovative artist, reaching number 21 on the Billboard 200 and featuring the title track that addressed social and political themes. The album's blend of new wave, funk, and rock influences, along with its controversial lyrical content, helped define Prince's artistic persona. Songs like "Sexuality" pushed boundaries and demonstrated his willingness to challenge social conventions through his music.

His major-label debut showcased Prince as a multi-instrumentalist who played all instruments and produced the entire album himself. The album reached number 22 on the Billboard 200 and featured the hit "I Wanna Be Your Lover," which became his first top 20 single. At just 21 years old, Prince demonstrated remarkable musical maturity and established the template for his future artistic independence and control.

This double album represented Prince's commercial breakthrough, reaching number 9 on the Billboard 200 and establishing him as a major force in popular music. The album featured multiple hit singles including the title track, "Little Red Corvette," and "Delirious," showcasing Prince's ability to blend rock, funk, and pop into a cohesive artistic statement. The release set the stage for his subsequent superstardom with Purple Rain.

Prince played the first of seven nights at the Joe Louis Arena in Detroit, Michigan at the start of his 87-date North American Purple Rain tour. The outing marked the live debut of his new band The Revolution.

Event: Cream goes #1 on US singles charts
Notes: This milestone marks when "Cream" became Prince's fifth number-one hit on the Billboard Hot 100, topping the charts for two weeks. The song, from the Diamonds and Pearls album, showcased Prince's ability to create irresistibly catchy pop-funk confections that dominated both pop and R&B charts. The track's success demonstrated his continued relevance in the rapidly changing musical landscape of the early 1990s.

Event: Rave Un2 The Joy Fantastic album released
Notes: This album marks a return to more accessible R&B and pop sounds after several experimental releases. The record peaked at number 18 on the Billboard 200 and featured collaborations with guest artists including Gwen Stefani on the title track. Critics praised the album's cohesive production and melodic sensibilities, viewing it as a creative renaissance during his later Arista Records period.

This ambitious triple-disc set celebrates artistic freedom following his departure from Warner Bros. Records. The 36-track opus peaked at number 11 on the Billboard 200 and included the hit single "Betcha By Golly, Wow!" The album's sprawling scope covered everything from funk and rock to ballads, representing his first major release as an independent artist.

This album reflects his deepening spiritual beliefs as a Jehovah's Witness, featuring religious themes throughout its jazz-fusion influenced tracks. Released exclusively through his NPG Music Club website, it marked an early experiment in direct-to-fan digital distribution. The record received mixed reviews but demonstrated his continued willingness to explore new musical territories and business models.

Originally recorded in 1987 but shelved until this official release, this funk album was legendary among bootleggers for years. The dark, sexually explicit record was initially pulled just before its scheduled release, with only a few promotional copies circulating. Its eventual official release revealed some of his most uncompromising and groove-heavy material from the late 1980s.

Born Sheila Cecelia Escovedo, she celebrates her birthday as one of the most accomplished percussionists in popular music. Her collaboration with Prince launched her solo career with hits like "The Glamorous Life" and "A Love Bizarre" in the mid-1980s. She brought Latin percussion influences to the Minneapolis sound and became a prominent figure in the funk and R&B scenes.

Released just months before his death, this album represents his final studio work and second release through the Tidal streaming platform. The record featured more introspective and politically conscious material, including tracks addressing social issues and personal reflection. It would tragically become a farewell statement from one of music's most prolific artists.

This album continued his exploration of jazz-fusion and spiritual themes, following "The Rainbow Children" with similarly complex musical arrangements. Released through his NPG Music Club, it featured lengthy instrumental passages and sophisticated harmonic progressions. The record demonstrated his continued evolution as a composer while maintaining his trademark genre-blending approach.

Born Park Jae-sang, he celebrates his birthday as the South Korean rapper who brought K-pop to global mainstream attention. His 2012 hit "Gangnam Style" became the first YouTube video to reach one billion views and topped charts worldwide. The viral sensation opened doors for Korean popular music in Western markets and established him as an international cultural ambassador.

Born William Jonathan Drayton Jr., he celebrates his birthday as Public Enemy's flamboyant hype man and cultural icon. His energetic stage presence, oversized clock necklaces, and catchphrase "Yeah boyeeeeee!" became signature elements of the group's live performances. Beyond his role as comic relief, he provided crucial balance to Chuck D's serious political messaging.

Event: It Takes A Nation Of Millions To Hold Us Back album released
Notes: This landmark album marks a milestone in hip-hop's artistic and political development, widely considered one of the greatest rap albums ever made. The record peaked at number 42 on the Billboard 200 but its cultural impact far exceeded its commercial performance. Tracks like "Bring the Noise" and "Don't Believe the Hype" showcased the Bomb Squad's revolutionary production techniques and Chuck D's powerful social commentary.

Born Carlton Douglas Ridenhour, he celebrates his birthday as one of hip-hop's most influential political voices and Public Enemy's commanding frontman. His deep, authoritative delivery and socially conscious lyrics helped establish rap as a vehicle for serious political discourse. He coined the phrase "rap is the CNN of the ghetto" and remains an active voice in social justice movements.

Born Norman Lee Rogers, he celebrates his birthday as Public Enemy's innovative DJ who helped define the group's sonic assault. His turntable skills and stage presence, including his signature spinning moves, made him a visual focal point during live performances. He contributed to the Bomb Squad's production team that created some of hip-hop's most complex and influential soundscapes.

This mogul celebrates his birthday as one of hip-hop's most successful entrepreneurs and producers who shaped 1990s rap and R&B. His Bad Boy Records launched careers of artists like The Notorious B.I.G. and Faith Evans while his own recording career produced hits like "I'll Be Missing You." He expanded beyond music into fashion, television, and spirits, becoming a blueprint for artist-turned-businessman success.

Born Tara Leigh Patrick, she celebrates her birthday as a model, actress, and dancer who became a pop culture icon of the 1990s and 2000s. Her appearances in "Baywatch" and various music videos established her as a sex symbol, while her brief association with the Pussycat Dolls connected her to the music world. She represented the era's crossover between modeling, television, and music entertainment.

This album marks an important step in Queen's evolution toward theatrical rock grandeur, featuring elaborate multi-tracking and operatic arrangements. The record peaked at number 5 in the UK and included "Seven Seas of Rhye," their first top 10 British hit. Brian May's guitar orchestrations and Freddie Mercury's vocal layering techniques established the signature Queen sound that would define their career.

Held at Wembley Stadium as a tribute to Freddie Mercury and for aids awareness. Artists appearing included Elton John, Roger Daltrey, Tony Iommi, David Bowie, Mick Ronson, James Hetfield, George Michael, Seal, Paul Young, Annie Lennox, Lisa Stansfield, Robert Plant, Joe Elliott and Phil Collen, Axl Rose and Slash.

This album represents Queen's controversial venture into disco and dance music, dividing fans and critics with its departure from their rock sound. Despite including the hit "Under Pressure" with David Bowie, the record received mixed reviews and lower sales compared to previous releases. The album's exploration of funk and electronic elements showed their willingness to experiment, though many consider it their least successful creative period.
They were billed as Smile and played at Truro City Hall, Cornwall, England

This album celebrates Queen's commercial peak in America, becoming their only number 1 album in the US and featuring two chart-topping singles. "Crazy Little Thing Called Love" and "Another One Bites the Dust" showcased their versatility across rockabilly and funk styles. The record's success established them as a major stadium act and demonstrated their ability to adapt to changing musical trends while maintaining their distinctive identity.

This debut album marks the beginning of one of rock's most legendary careers, introducing the world to Freddie Mercury's powerful vocals and the band's heavy guitar sound. While it didn't achieve major commercial success initially, tracks like "Keep Yourself Alive" established their signature multi-layered approach to rock music. The album laid the foundation for their evolution into one of the most theatrical and influential bands in rock history.

Born Brian Harold May in Hampton, this astrophysicist-turned-guitarist became one of rock's most distinctive players with his homemade Red Special guitar and symphonic approach to layering. His intricate harmonies and orchestral guitar arrangements helped define Queen's grandiose sound on classics like "Bohemian Rhapsody" and "We Will Rock You." Beyond music, he completed his PhD in astrophysics and became a passionate advocate for animal rights.

Born Roger Meddows Taylor in Norfolk, he provided the powerhouse drumming and soaring harmonies that became essential to Queen's signature sound. His multi-octave vocal range complemented Freddie Mercury's lead vocals on hits like "Radio Ga Ga" and "A Kind of Magic," while his songwriting contributed fan favorites including "I'm in Love with My Car." Taylor also pursued a successful solo career and remains active in music production.

Born Farrokh Bulsara in Stone Town, Zanzibar, he transformed into one of rock's most theatrical and powerful frontmen. His four-octave vocal range and flamboyant stage presence made Queen's live performances legendary, culminating in their show-stealing Live Aid performance in 1985. Mercury's songwriting genius produced timeless anthems like "Bohemian Rhapsody," "We Are the Champions," and "Somebody to Love" before his death from AIDS-related complications in 1991.

This breakthrough third album marked Queen's emergence as stadium rock royalty, reaching #2 in the UK and establishing their signature blend of hard rock and operatic grandeur. The album spawned the hit "Killer Queen," which became their first top 10 single in Britain and showcased Freddie Mercury's theatrical songwriting style. Recorded at various studios including Trident and Air, the album's layered production techniques would become a Queen trademark.

Event: A Night at the Opera album released
Notes: This ambitious fourth album represents Queen's creative peak, featuring the groundbreaking six-minute epic "Bohemian Rhapsody" which became their first UK #1 hit. The album topped charts worldwide and demonstrated the band's fearless genre-blending, from the mock-opera of the title track to the folk balladry of "'39" and hard rock of "Stone Cold Crazy." Its expensive production costs nearly bankrupted their label, but the artistic risks paid off with both critical acclaim and massive commercial success.

Event: A Day At The Races album released
Notes: Following the massive success of "A Night at the Opera," Queen delivered this equally ambitious sequel that continued their exploration of musical theater and rock opera. The album featured hits like "Somebody to Love" and "Tie Your Mother Down," reaching #1 in the UK and solidifying their reputation for elaborate, multi-layered productions. Recorded primarily at Sarm Studios and The Manor, it showcased the band's growing confidence in blending genres from gospel to heavy metal.
Singer, guitarist from Free, Bad Company, toured with Queen

This versatile guitarist and multi-instrumentalist became QOTSA's secret weapon, joining the band in 2002 and contributing to their most acclaimed albums. His previous work with A Perfect Circle and Failure brought a melodic sensibility that complemented Josh Homme's desert rock vision. Van Leeuwen's guitar work and backing vocals helped define the band's sound on classics like "No One Knows" and "Go With the Flow."

Born Joshua Michael Homme III, this Palm Desert native founded Queens of the Stone Age from the ashes of stoner rock pioneers Kyuss. His distinctive downtuned guitar style and crooning vocals created a new template for desert rock, producing modern classics like "No One Knows" and "Little Sister." Homme's collaborative spirit has made him one of alternative rock's most sought-after producers and musicians.

Born Quincy Delight Jones Jr. in Chicago, he became one of the most successful producers and arrangers in popular music history. His work with Michael Jackson on "Off the Wall," "Thriller," and "Bad" redefined pop music, while his jazz arrangements and film scores earned him 28 Grammy Awards. Jones's influence spans decades and genres, from his early work with Count Basie and Frank Sinatra to launching the careers of countless R&B and pop stars.
R.E.M. singer

R.E.M.'s sophomore album refined their jangly alternative sound while maintaining the mysterious, impressionistic lyrics that made "Murmur" so compelling. The album reached #27 on the Billboard 200 and featured college radio favorites like "So. Central Rain" and "Don't Go Back to Rockville." Recorded at Reflection Sound Studios in North Carolina, it solidified their reputation as leaders of the emerging American underground rock scene.

This stunning debut album launched R.E.M. from Athens, Georgia obscurity to critical acclaim, ranking #1 on Rolling Stone's year-end list and establishing the template for alternative rock. Michael Stipe's cryptic, mumbled vocals and Peter Buck's chiming Rickenbacker guitar created an atmospheric sound that influenced countless indie bands. Songs like "Radio Free Europe" and "Talk About the Passion" became college radio staples and helped define the sound of the 1980s underground.

This politically charged fourth album marked R.E.M.'s growing confidence as songwriters and social commentators, reaching #21 on the Billboard 200. Featuring more direct lyrics than previous efforts, songs like "Fall on Me" addressed environmental concerns while "Superman" showed their playful side. The album's cleaner production and Stipe's increasingly intelligible vocals signaled their transition from cult college rock heroes to mainstream alternative pioneers.

R.E.M.'s fifth studio album became their commercial breakthrough, reaching #10 on the Billboard 200 and earning platinum certification. The hit single "The One I Love" gave them their first top 10 single, while "It's the End of the World as We Know It (And I Feel Fine)" became a cultural touchstone. Recorded at Sound Emporium in Nashville, the album's political themes and improved production helped establish alternative rock as a commercial force.

Event: Automatic For The People album released
Notes: This introspective masterpiece became R.E.M.'s biggest commercial success, topping charts worldwide and selling over 18 million copies. The album spawned massive hits including "Losing My Religion," "Shiny Happy People," and the haunting "Everybody Hurts," showcasing the band's emotional range and Michael Stipe's most personal songwriting. Recorded at Bearsville Studios, its themes of mortality and reflection resonated globally during a turbulent period in world history.

Born Peter Lawrence Buck, this influential guitarist's jangly Rickenbacker sound became the backbone of R.E.M.'s signature style and helped define alternative rock. His melodic, arpeggiated playing on classics like "Losing My Religion" and "The One I Love" influenced a generation of indie musicians. Buck's minimalist approach and emphasis on texture over technique made him one of the most imitated guitarists of the 1980s and 1990s.
Bass player from R.E.M.

Radiohead's debut album introduced the world to their brand of guitar-driven alternative rock, though it barely hinted at their future experimental direction. The album reached #25 in the UK primarily on the strength of "Creep," which became a worldwide hit despite initially being dismissed by the band themselves. Recorded at Chipping Norton Studios, the album's grunge-influenced sound reflected the era while Thom Yorke's alienated lyrics suggested the innovative artistry to come.

Born Edward John O'Brien, this guitarist and backing vocalist became essential to Radiohead's evolution from alternative rock to experimental pioneers. His innovative use of effects pedals and electronic manipulation helped create the atmospheric soundscapes on albums like "OK Computer" and "Kid A." O'Brien's contributions extend beyond guitar work to include sampling, programming, and the textural elements that define Radiohead's later catalog.
Bass player

This groundbreaking fourth album marked a radical departure from the band's guitar-driven sound, embracing electronic music, sampling, and ambient textures. The album debuted at number one in the UK and reached number 62 on the Billboard 200, while tracks like "Everything In Its Right Place" and "Idioteque" redefined what rock music could be in the new millennium. Recorded using unconventional methods including cutting up recordings and drawing chord progressions from a hat, Kid A divided fans initially but is now widely regarded as one of the most influential albums of the 2000s.

Born Thomas Edward Yorke, he celebrates another year as one of alternative rock's most distinctive and influential voices. As the frontman and primary songwriter for Radiohead, he helped craft genre-defining albums like OK Computer and Kid A, while his falsetto vocals and politically charged lyrics became signature elements of the band's sound. His solo work and side project Atoms for Peace have further established him as an innovative artist unafraid to push musical boundaries.
The first 'pay-what-you-want' release for a major act

Event: Kid A hits #1 on US album charts
Notes: The group's fourth album became the first Radiohead release to debut at No.1 in the US.
Guitarist, keyboard player

Born Zacharias Manuel de la Rocha, he celebrates another year as one of rap-rock's most powerful and politically charged voices. As the frontman of Rage Against The Machine, his fiery delivery and revolutionary lyrics on tracks like "Killing in the Name" and "Bulls on Parade" helped define protest music for the 1990s. His ability to seamlessly blend hip-hop vocals with heavy rock instrumentation created a template that influenced countless bands and cemented his status as a voice for social justice in music.

The bassist celebrates another year as the rhythmic backbone of one of the most politically charged bands in rock history. His aggressive, effects-heavy bass work provided the foundation for Rage Against The Machine's revolutionary sound, particularly evident on explosive tracks like "Bulls on Parade" and "Testify." His collaboration with drummer Brad Wilk created one of the tightest rhythm sections in alternative metal, helping the band achieve both critical acclaim and commercial success throughout the 1990s.

Born Thomas Baptiste Morello, he celebrates another year as one of rock's most innovative and recognizable guitarists. His groundbreaking use of effects pedals, unconventional techniques, and ability to make his guitar sound like a DJ's turntable revolutionized heavy music and earned him a spot on Rolling Stone's list of greatest guitarists. Beyond Rage Against The Machine, his work with Audioslave and as The Nightwatchman has showcased both his musical versatility and commitment to social activism.

The drummer celebrates another year as the percussive powerhouse behind some of the most explosive protest anthems in rock history. His precise, hard-hitting style provided the foundation for Rage Against The Machine's genre-defying sound, while his later work with Audioslave proved his versatility across different musical contexts. His ability to lock in with bassist Tim Commerford created a rhythm section that could match the intensity of Tom Morello's innovative guitar work and Zack de la Rocha's fiery vocals.

Event: Rage Against The Machine album released
Notes: This explosive debut album revolutionized the intersection of metal, punk, and hip-hop, introducing the world to the band's unique brand of politically charged rock. The album reached number 45 on the Billboard 200 and eventually achieved triple-platinum status, with incendiary tracks like "Killing in the Name" and "Bombtrack" becoming anthems of rebellion. Tom Morello's innovative guitar techniques, combined with Zack de la Rocha's rap-influenced vocals, created a sound that was both musically groundbreaking and politically urgent, influencing countless bands and establishing the template for rap-rock.

This live recording session captured the North Carolina indie rock band at a pivotal moment in their rising career, showcasing their intimate acoustic arrangements and Sam Melo's distinctive vocals. The performance featured fan favorites like "It's Called: Freefall" and "Devil Like Me," demonstrating the band's ability to translate their studio sound into compelling live performances. The Audiotree session helped expand their fanbase and marked an important step in their journey from college town favorites to nationally recognized indie rock acts.

Event: How To: Friend, Love, Freefall album released
Notes: This major label debut marked the North Carolina band's breakthrough into mainstream success, reaching number 18 on the Billboard 200 and earning gold certification. The album featured the hit single "It's Called: Freefall" and showcased the band's evolution from indie folk to a more polished alternative rock sound. Sam Melo's vulnerable lyrics about love, identity, and mental health resonated with a growing fanbase, while the band's dynamic arrangements demonstrated their musical growth since their independent releases.

This self-titled album represented a significant step forward for the North Carolina indie rock band, showcasing a more refined sound and production quality. The release helped establish their growing reputation beyond their regional fanbase, featuring tracks that would become concert staples and demonstrating the band's evolution from their earlier folk-influenced work. The album served as an important bridge between their indie beginnings and their eventual major label success.

This debut album introduced the world to the North Carolina band's unique blend of indie folk and alternative rock, built around Sam Melo's distinctive vocals and introspective songwriting. The independent release captured the raw energy and intimate sound that first attracted their college town following, laying the groundwork for their future success. Though recorded on a modest budget, the album showcased the band's potential and began building the dedicated fanbase that would support their rise to national prominence.

Event: Rainbow Kitten Surprise formed in North Carloina in early 2013
Notes: The indie rock band formed in Boone, North Carolina, when college students came together to create their distinctive blend of folk, rock, and alternative sounds. Founded around the songwriting partnership that would become central to their identity, the group began performing in local venues and quickly developed a following in the college town music scene. Their early formation marked the beginning of what would become a journey from regional favorites to nationally recognized artists with a dedicated fanbase.

The towering frontman celebrates another year as one of metal's most commanding and theatrical performers. His deep, operatic voice and imposing stage presence helped make Rammstein one of the most successful German bands internationally, while his lyrics in German brought the language to global metal audiences. His background as a competitive swimmer and his training in theatrical performance have contributed to the band's elaborate live shows, making him an iconic figure in industrial metal.

The bassist celebrates another year as part of the rhythmic foundation of Germany's most internationally successful metal export. His precise, heavy bass work has been crucial to Rammstein's industrial metal sound since the band's formation in the mid-1990s, helping drive explosive tracks like "Du Hast" and "Sonne." His contributions to the band's distinctive sound have helped them achieve both critical acclaim in Europe and crossover success in international markets.

The drummer celebrates another year providing the thunderous backbone to one of Germany's most successful metal acts. His powerful, precise drumming has been essential to Rammstein's industrial metal sound, creating the rhythmic foundation for their explosive live performances and stadium-filling anthems. His work behind the kit has helped the band achieve massive international success while maintaining their distinctly German identity in the global metal scene.

Born Richard Zven Kruspe-Bernstein, the guitarist and founding member celebrates another year as the musical architect behind Germany's most internationally successful metal band. His heavy, industrial guitar sound and songwriting have been central to Rammstein's distinctive style, helping create anthems like "Du Hast" that brought German-language metal to global audiences. His vision for combining heavy guitars with electronic elements and theatrical performances helped establish the band's unique identity in the industrial metal landscape.

Born Paul Heiko Landers, the guitarist celebrates his birthday today as one of the founding members of German industrial metal giants Rammstein. His heavy, precise riffing helped define the band's bombastic sound on classics like "Du Hast" and "Sonne." Landers' theatrical stage presence and pyrotechnic performances made Rammstein one of the most visually spectacular live acts in metal history.

The punk pioneers' tenth studio album marked a brief return to form after several commercially disappointing releases. Produced by Bill Laswell, the record featured the band's last notable radio hit "Pet Sematary," written for the Stephen King film adaptation. While it didn't match their classic mid-70s output, Brain Drain proved the Ramones could still deliver catchy three-chord anthems late in their career.

Event: Too Tough to Die album released
Notes: This comeback album marked a creative resurgence for the punk legends after lineup changes and personal struggles nearly derailed the band. Featuring new drummer Richie Ramone, the record delivered some of their strongest material in years with tracks like "Mama's Boy" and "Howling at the Moon." The album's title proved prophetic as the Ramones demonstrated their remarkable resilience and influence on the emerging hardcore punk scene.

Born Randy Bruce Traywick in North Carolina, this country legend celebrates his birthday as one of the genre's most distinctive baritone voices. His 1986 breakthrough "Storms of Life" helped usher in the neo-traditional country movement, while classics like "Forever and Ever, Amen" and "On the Other Hand" showcased his ability to blend honky-tonk tradition with contemporary appeal. His rich vocals and authentic storytelling made him one of country music's most respected artists of the 1980s and 1990s.

The country trio's seventh studio album represented a sonic shift toward a more pop-rock influenced sound. The record spawned the hit singles "Banjo" and "Come Wake Me Up," with the latter reaching the top 20 on country radio. Despite mixed critical reception, Changed demonstrated the band's willingness to experiment with their established harmonious formula after more than a decade of mainstream success.

Event: Me and My Gang album released
Notes: This powerhouse album became the trio's fastest-selling release, debuting at number one on both country and pop charts. The record produced four consecutive number-one country hits including "What Hurts the Most," which became their biggest crossover success. The album's blend of country harmonies with pop sensibilities helped solidify their status as one of country music's premier vocal groups of the 2000s.

The group's sixth studio album continued their streak of chart-topping success, debuting at number one on the country charts. Lead single "Here Comes Goodbye" became their tenth number-one hit, while the album showcased their signature three-part harmonies on both uptempo anthems and emotional ballads. The record proved their commercial dominance remained intact well into their second decade together.

Born Jay Stanley DeMarcus, the bassist and keyboardist celebrates his birthday as the versatile musician behind some of country music's most recognizable harmonies. His production work and musical arrangements helped define the Rascal Flatts sound that dominated country radio throughout the 2000s. DeMarcus also pursued a successful career as a producer for other country acts while maintaining his role in the trio.

The trio's eighth studio album marked their return after a brief hiatus, debuting at number one on the country charts. The record featured the nostalgic title track "Rewind" and the uplifting "Payback," both of which reached the country top 20. The album's themes of looking back and moving forward seemed to capture the band's reflective mood as they entered their third decade together.

This tenth studio album represented a more personal and introspective direction for the veteran trio. The record featured collaborations with various songwriters and a more stripped-down production approach compared to their earlier pop-country sound. While it didn't achieve the massive commercial success of their peak years, Back to Us showcased the group's matured songwriting and enduring vocal chemistry.

The trio's major-label debut introduced their distinctive three-part harmonies to country radio with immediate impact. The self-titled album produced three top-10 hits including "Prayin' for Daylight" and "I'm Movin' On," establishing their signature blend of country storytelling and pop accessibility. This breakthrough release launched one of country music's most successful vocal groups of the new millennium.

Born Gary Wayne Vernon Jr. in Columbus, Ohio, the lead vocalist celebrates his birthday as the powerful voice behind countless country radio hits. His soaring tenor anchored the trio's signature sound on classics like "Bless the Broken Road" and "Life is a Highway." LeVox's dynamic vocal range and emotional delivery helped make him one of country music's most recognizable frontmen of the 2000s.

Born in Baxter Springs, Kansas, the guitarist celebrates his birthday as the instrumental backbone of one of country's most successful trios. His guitar work and backing vocals provided the perfect complement to the group's polished harmonies throughout their chart-dominating run. Rooney's musicianship helped craft the signature Rascal Flatts sound that defined mainstream country music for over two decades.

The group's fifth studio album continued their remarkable commercial streak, debuting at number one and producing multiple chart-toppers. Lead single "Take Me There" showcased their ability to blend country themes with arena-rock production, while "Better Than a Memory" demonstrated their ballad prowess. The record reinforced their position as country music's premier harmony-driven act during their peak commercial period.

This fourth studio album produced some of the trio's most enduring hits, including the inspirational "Bless the Broken Road" which became their signature song. The album also featured "Fast Cars and Freedom" and "Skin (Sarabeth)," showcasing their range from uptempo rockers to deeply emotional ballads. The record's success helped establish them as country music's dominant vocal group of the mid-2000s.

Event: Twenty Years of Rascal Flatts: The Greatest Hits album released
Notes: This comprehensive career retrospective celebrated the trio's remarkable two-decade run as country music's most successful vocal group. The collection featured all their biggest hits alongside three new tracks, serving as both a farewell gift to fans and a monument to their chart dominance. Released as the group entered an indefinite hiatus, the album marked the end of an era in contemporary country music.

Event: The Greatest Gift of All album released
Notes: The trio's Christmas album showcased their signature harmonies on both traditional carols and contemporary holiday songs. This seasonal offering allowed them to explore different musical textures while maintaining their polished country-pop sound. The record demonstrated their versatility beyond mainstream country while providing fans with a festive soundtrack featuring their beloved three-part vocals.

This compilation captured the trio at their commercial peak, featuring 16 of their biggest hits including seven number-one singles. The collection showcased their evolution from country newcomers to mainstream superstars with classics like "What Hurts the Most" and "My Wish." The album served as a perfect introduction to their catalog and a celebration of their unprecedented chart success throughout the 2000s.

The trio's sophomore album solidified their status as country music's rising stars with a more polished production and stronger songwriting. The record produced massive hits including "These Days" and "Love You Out Loud," both of which topped the country charts. Melt's success proved their debut wasn't a fluke and established the template for their future commercial dominance.

The group's seventh studio album marked another number-one debut and continued their string of chart successes into the new decade. Lead single "Why Wait" became their fifteenth top-10 hit, while the album's production maintained their signature blend of country harmonies and pop sensibilities. The record proved their commercial appeal remained strong even as country music's landscape continued to evolve around them.

Today marks the birthday of Ray Charles Robinson, who became one of the most influential musicians of the 20th century. He pioneered the fusion of gospel, blues, and R&B into what became known as soul music, earning him the nickname 'The Genius.' His groundbreaking approach to blending sacred and secular music revolutionized popular music and influenced countless artists across all genres.

Event: Georgia On My Mind goes #1 on US singles charts
Notes: Today celebrates the moment when Ray Charles' rendition of 'Georgia On My Mind' reached the top of the US singles charts, becoming his first #1 pop hit. The song, originally written by Hoagy Carmichael and Stuart Gorrell in 1930, was transformed by Charles into a deeply soulful masterpiece that would later become Georgia's official state song. This achievement marked Charles' crossover from R&B success to mainstream pop stardom.

This live album captures performances from LaMontagne's tour supporting his acclaimed 'God Willin' and the Creek Don't Rise' album. The release showcased his intimate acoustic style and powerful vocals in a concert setting. It served as a bridge between his Dan Auerbach-produced studio album and his evolving sound.

LaMontagne's sixth studio album marked a bold departure into psychedelic rock territory, featuring his band The Pariah Dogs. The album was entirely self-produced and represented his most experimental work to date. Critics praised its dreamy, atmospheric sound while noting its departure from his signature folk style.

This fifth studio album saw LaMontagne collaborating with members of My Morning Jacket as his backing band The Pariah Dogs. The album blended his folk roots with more rock-oriented arrangements and psychedelic influences. It peaked at #18 on the Billboard 200 and marked a creative evolution in his sound.

Event: Part of the Light album released
Notes: LaMontagne's seventh studio album returned to a more intimate, acoustic-driven sound after his experimental phase. The album was recorded at his home studio in Massachusetts and featured a more personal, introspective collection of songs. It debuted at #17 on the Billboard 200, proving his enduring appeal across different musical styles.

This early career EP captured LaMontagne's breakout performance at the renowned Tennessee music festival. Released just one year after his debut album 'Trouble,' it showcased his powerful live presence and helped establish his reputation as a compelling performer. The recording highlighted his ability to connect with audiences through his emotionally raw vocals.

Today marks the birthday of Raymond Charles Jack LaMontagne, who became one of the most distinctive voices in contemporary folk and indie rock. His 2004 debut album 'Trouble' launched a career characterized by his raspy, soulful vocals and introspective songwriting. He's known for his reclusive nature and authentic approach to music-making that has earned him both critical acclaim and a devoted fanbase.

Event: God Willin' and the Creek Don't Rise album released
Notes: LaMontagne's fourth studio album was produced by Dan Auerbach of The Black Keys and marked a significant evolution in his sound. The album incorporated more electric instruments and fuller arrangements while maintaining his signature emotional intensity. It debuted at #3 on the Billboard 200, becoming his highest-charting album at the time.

This second studio album continued to build on the success of his debut 'Trouble' with its melancholic folk-rock sound. The album featured standout tracks like 'Three More Days' and the title track, showcasing LaMontagne's gift for crafting deeply emotional songs. It solidified his reputation as a masterful songwriter and vocalist in the indie folk scene.

LaMontagne's debut album became a critical and commercial success, establishing him as a major new voice in folk music. The title track 'Trouble' became his signature song and received significant radio play and film soundtrack placements. The album's raw, emotional honesty and his distinctive raspy vocals immediately set him apart in the contemporary folk landscape.

Event: Gossip in the Grain album released
Notes: This third studio album showcased a more refined and polished sound while maintaining LaMontagne's emotional core. The album featured lush orchestrations and continued his exploration of themes around love, loss, and redemption. It demonstrated his growth as a songwriter and his ability to evolve his sound while staying true to his artistic vision.

This second live album captured performances from LaMontagne's fall tour, showcasing material from across his catalog. The recording highlighted his continued growth as a live performer and featured intimate versions of fan favorites. It served as a companion piece to his earlier spring live release from the same year.
The invention made jukeboxes possible

Event: Sing It Now: Songs of Faith & Hope album released
Notes: This double album marked McEntire's return to her gospel and spiritual roots with a collection of hymns and contemporary Christian songs. The project showcased her versatility beyond country music and reflected her deep faith. It was her first studio album in five years and demonstrated her enduring vocal power and emotional range.

This self-titled album represented a significant step in McEntire's artistic development as she gained more creative control over her music. The album featured a more polished production style and showcased her evolving vocal maturity. It helped establish her transition from traditional country toward a more contemporary sound.

Event: Whoever's in New England album released
Notes: This breakthrough album established McEntire as a major country star and earned her first Grammy Award for Best Female Country Vocal Performance. The title track became a signature song and demonstrated her storytelling abilities with its narrative about marital suspicion and heartbreak. The album marked her emergence as one of country music's premier female voices.

Today celebrates the birthday of Reba Nell McEntire, who became known as 'The Queen of Country Music' through her powerful vocals and dynamic stage presence. She has sold over 75 million records worldwide and is one of the best-selling music artists of all time. Her career spans music, television, and Broadway, making her one of entertainment's most versatile and enduring stars.

Event: Just a Little Love album released
Notes: This album marked an important step in McEntire's career development, featuring more contemporary country production styles. It helped bridge her early traditional country sound with the more polished approach that would define her later success. The album showcased her growing confidence as a vocalist and interpreter of songs.

Event: Stronger Than the Truth album released
Notes: McEntire's 29th studio album returned to her traditional country roots with a collection of songs emphasizing authentic storytelling and classic country instrumentation. The album was praised for its return to form and featured themes of resilience and truth-telling. It demonstrated that even decades into her career, she remained a vital and relevant artist in country music.

Marks the release of her 33rd studio album, featuring the title track that became a moderate country radio hit. The album represented a return to traditional country sounds after her previous pop-leaning efforts and showcased her enduring vocal power at age 60.

Celebrates the release of her breakthrough album that launched her into superstardom with hits like 'I Know How He Feels' and 'New Fool at an Old Game.' The album marked her transition from traditional country to a more contemporary sound and established her as one of country music's leading ladies.

Marks the release of her 20th studio album, featuring the hit singles 'Why Haven't I Heard from You' and 'She Thinks His Name Was John,' the latter being one of the first mainstream country songs to address AIDS. The album peaked at number 2 on the country charts and demonstrated her willingness to tackle serious social issues.

Celebrates the release of her follow-up to the massively successful 'Reba' album, featuring hits like 'Cathy's Clown' and 'Walk On.' The album continued her hot streak of the late 1980s and solidified her position as one of country music's biggest stars.

Event: If You See Him album released
Notes: Marks the release of her collaboration album with Brooks & Dunn, featuring the crossover hit title track that topped both country and adult contemporary charts. The album showcased her versatility in duet partnerships and proved her continued relevance in the changing country landscape of the late 1990s.

Event: Have I Got a Deal for You album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of her ninth studio album, which included the hit single 'Have I Got a Deal for You' and marked her continued evolution toward a more polished, mainstream country sound. The album helped bridge the gap between her earlier traditional style and the breakthrough success that would come with later releases.

Marks the release of her third studio album for Mercury Records, featuring a more contemporary country-pop sound that would become her trademark. The album included several charting singles and represented an important step in her artistic development during the early 1980s.

Celebrates the release of her major-label debut album on Mercury Records, launching one of country music's most successful careers. The album featured her first charting single 'I Don't Want to Be a One Night Stand' and established the foundation for what would become a legendary recording career spanning over four decades.

Marks the release of her sixth studio album, which continued to build her reputation as a rising country star with hits like 'Why Do We Want (What We Know We Can't Have).' The album showcased her developing vocal maturity and helped establish her as a consistent chart presence in country music.

Celebrates the release of her second major-label album, featuring the hit single 'Today All Over Again' which became her first top 5 country hit. The album marked a significant step forward in her career and helped establish her distinctive vocal style that would make her a country music icon.

Event: Keep on Loving You album released
Notes: Marks the release of her first album for the Valory Music Group, featuring the hit single 'Strange' and marking her return to a more traditional country sound. The album celebrated her enduring career while proving she could still compete with younger artists in the contemporary country market.

Event: Out of a Dream album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of her second studio album, which featured the charting single 'Last Night, Ev'ry Night' and continued to develop her signature vocal style. The album helped establish her growing reputation in Nashville and set the stage for her breakthrough success in the early 1980s.

Marks the release of her first live album, capturing the energy and charisma that made her one of country music's most popular concert performers. The album featured live versions of her biggest hits and demonstrated why she had become known as one of the genre's most dynamic live acts.

Event: My Kind of Christmas album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of her third Christmas album, featuring a mix of traditional holiday classics and contemporary seasonal songs. The album showcased her enduring vocal power and continued relevance in country music, proving her ability to bring warmth and authenticity to beloved holiday material.

Marks the release of one of her most successful albums, spawning four number one hits including 'You Lie,' 'Rumor Has It,' and 'Fancy.' The album reached number one on the country charts and established her as the undisputed 'Queen of Country Music' during her commercial peak.

Event: The Last One to Know album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of her highly successful album featuring three number one hits including 'Love Will Find Its Way to You' and the title track. The album continued her remarkable chart dominance of the late 1980s and further cemented her status as country music's reigning female superstar.

Marks the release of her collaborative album featuring duets with artists like Kelly Clarkson, Carrie Underwood, and Kenny Chesney on 'Every Other Weekend.' The album celebrated her influence on younger artists and demonstrated her continued relevance across multiple generations of country music.

Event: The Secret of Giving: A Christmas Collection album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of her second Christmas album, featuring traditional holiday songs and the touching original 'The Secret of Giving.' The album showcased her ability to bring emotional depth to seasonal material and became a holiday favorite for country music fans.

Event: For My Broken Heart album released
Notes: Marks the release of her most emotionally powerful album, created in the aftermath of the tragic plane crash that killed her band members. The album featured the deeply personal title track and 'Is There Life Out There,' both reaching number one and earning critical acclaim for their raw emotional honesty.

Celebrates the release of her comeback album after a brief hiatus, featuring hits like 'On My Own' and the title track. The album marked her return to form and proved her enduring popularity, reaching number one on the country charts and re-establishing her chart dominance.

Celebrates the release of Feel the Fire, McEntire's second studio album that marked an early step in her evolution from traditional country towards a more contemporary sound. The album featured the single "I Can See Forever in Your Eyes" and was produced by Jerry Kennedy. While it didn't achieve major commercial success, it helped establish her growing reputation in Nashville's country music scene.

Event: What Am I Gonna Do About You album released
Notes: Marks the release of What Am I Gonna Do About You, McEntire's eleventh studio album that became a pivotal moment in her career. The album reached number 8 on the country charts and produced four Top 10 country singles, including the title track and "One Promise Too Late." This release solidified her transition into a more pop-influenced country sound that would define much of her later success.

Celebrates the release of Revived Remixed Revisited, a unique project that reimagined some of McEntire's classic hits with contemporary production and guest collaborations. The album featured updated versions of songs like "Does He Love You" with Dolly Parton and showed the country legend's willingness to experiment with her catalog. It demonstrated her enduring relevance in modern country music at age 66.

Event: My Kind of Country album released
Notes: Marks the release of My Kind of Country, the album that truly launched McEntire into country superstardom. The record reached number 13 on the country charts and spawned the number one hit "How Blue," along with other top singles like "Somebody Should Leave." Produced by Harold Shedd, it marked her shift toward a more traditional, emotionally-driven country sound that became her signature.

Event: What If It's You album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of What If It's You, McEntire's twenty-second studio album that reached number 15 on the country charts. The album produced several hit singles including "The Fear of Being Alone" and "How Was I to Know," showcasing her continued ability to blend traditional country storytelling with contemporary production. It arrived during her peak period of crossover success in the mid-1990s.

Event: All the Women I Am album released
Notes: Marks the release of All the Women I Am, McEntire's twenty-seventh studio album that debuted at number 3 on the country charts. The album featured the hit singles "Strange" and "If I Were a Boy" and demonstrated her continued relevance in country music well into her fifth decade of recording. The project showcased her ability to adapt to contemporary country trends while maintaining her distinctive vocal style.

Celebrates the release of Room to Breathe, an album that found McEntire exploring a more pop-country direction during her time with MCA Nashville. The record reached number 25 on the country charts and featured the single "Somebody," co-written by Brad Paisley. It represented her continued evolution as an artist while maintaining the emotional depth that made her a country icon.

Marks the release of So Good Together, McEntire's twenty-fifth studio album that reached number 28 on the country charts. The album included the singles "What Do You Say" and "I'll Be" and showcased her versatility with both ballads and uptempo tracks. It arrived during a period when she was balancing her music career with her successful television sitcom "Reba."

Event: Merry Christmas to You album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of Merry Christmas to You, McEntire's first Christmas album that became a holiday staple for country music fans. The album featured a mix of traditional carols and contemporary holiday songs, showcasing her warm vocal delivery on seasonal favorites. It established her as an artist who could successfully navigate different musical territories beyond mainstream country.

Marks the release of It's Your Call, an album that reached number 2 on the country charts and spawned several major hits including the title track and "The Heart Won't Lie," a duet with Vince Gill. The album demonstrated McEntire's peak commercial power in the early 1990s and featured some of her most emotionally resonant performances. It solidified her status as one of country music's premier female vocalists.

Celebrates the birth of John Anthony Frusciante, the innovative guitarist whose distinctive style helped define the Red Hot Chili Peppers' most successful era. His melodic, funk-influenced guitar work on albums like "Blood Sugar Sex Magik" and "Californication" elevated the band from alternative rock cult heroes to mainstream superstars. Despite leaving and rejoining the band multiple times, he remains one of the most influential guitarists of the alternative rock era.

Marks the birth of Hillel Slovak, the Red Hot Chili Peppers' original guitarist whose innovative fusion of punk, funk, and rock helped establish the band's groundbreaking sound. His playing on early albums like "Freaky Styley" and "The Uplift Mofo Party Plan" laid the foundation for the band's unique musical identity. Slovak's tragic death from a heroin overdose in 1988 deeply impacted the band and influenced their later advocacy for drug awareness.

Celebrates the release of Californication, the album that marked the Red Hot Chili Peppers' triumphant return to form and massive commercial breakthrough. The record reached number 3 on the Billboard 200 and spawned hit singles like "Scar Tissue," "Californication," and "Otherside." Recorded with producer Rick Rubin and featuring John Frusciante's return to the band, it perfectly balanced their funk-rock energy with more melodic songwriting.

Event: Blood Sugar Sex Magik album released
Notes: Marks the release of Blood Sugar Sex Magik, the breakthrough album that catapulted the Red Hot Chili Peppers to international stardom. Recorded in a mansion with producer Rick Rubin, the album reached number 3 on the Billboard 200 and featured massive hits like "Under the Bridge" and "Give It Away." The record perfectly captured the band's unique blend of funk, rap, and rock, establishing them as one of the most innovative acts of the 1990s.
Born Michael Balzary

Celebrates the birth of Chad Gaylord Smith, the powerhouse drummer who joined the Red Hot Chili Peppers in 1988 and became integral to their most successful period. His dynamic, hard-hitting style provided the rhythmic foundation for classics like "Under the Bridge" and "Californication." Smith's tenure with the band spans over three decades and includes their biggest commercial successes and critical acclaim.

Marks the birth of Anthony Kiedis, the charismatic frontman and primary lyricist who co-founded the Red Hot Chili Peppers in 1983. His distinctive vocal style, blending rap, melody, and spoken word, helped define the band's genre-bending sound across multiple decades. Kiedis's personal struggles and artistic growth became central themes in the band's music, from early party anthems to more introspective later works.

Event: You Can Tune a Piano, but You Can't Tuna Fish album released
Notes: Celebrates the release of You Can Tune a Piano, but You Can't Tuna Fish, REO Speedwagon's seventh studio album that marked their transition toward the arena rock sound that would make them superstars. The album reached number 29 on the Billboard 200 and featured the hit single "Roll with the Changes." It served as a crucial stepping stone toward their massive breakthrough with "Hi Infidelity" just two years later.

Marks the launch of Revolver Magazine, which became one of the most influential publications covering heavy metal, hard rock, and alternative music. Founded during the nu-metal boom, the magazine quickly established itself as a premier source for coverage of both established and emerging heavy music acts. Revolver filled a crucial gap in music journalism by focusing specifically on heavier genres often overlooked by mainstream publications.

Celebrates the birth of Alexander James O'Connor, the English singer-songwriter who performs as Rex Orange County and became a standout voice in indie pop and bedroom pop. His breakthrough came with albums like "Apricot Princess" and "Pony," which showcased his ability to blend jazz-influenced arrangements with intimate, confessional lyrics. O'Connor's music has resonated particularly strongly with younger audiences seeking authentic, emotionally honest pop music.

Born Richard Noel Marx, this Chicago native celebrates another year as one of the most successful singer-songwriters of the late 80s and 90s. Marx dominated adult contemporary radio with hits like "Right Here Waiting" and "Now and Forever," while also penning chart-toppers for other artists including "This I Promise You" for *NSYNC. His ability to craft both power ballads and uptempo rockers made him a fixture on both pop and rock charts throughout his career.

Born Richard Pierce Havens in Brooklyn, this folk singer-guitarist became a counterculture icon through his legendary opening performance at Woodstock in 1969. Havens captivated the festival crowd for nearly three hours with his distinctive rhythmic guitar style and passionate vocals, including his improvised "Freedom" chant that became one of the festival's most memorable moments. His career spanned five decades, during which he recorded over 20 albums and remained a powerful voice for social justice and artistic freedom.

Born Richard Paul Astley in Newton-le-Willows, England, this pop sensation celebrates another year since becoming an unlikely internet phenomenon decades after his initial fame. Astley dominated the late 80s with his debut album "Whenever You Need Somebody" and the massive hit "Never Gonna Give You Up," which later gained new life as the centerpiece of the "Rickrolling" meme. His distinctively deep, soulful voice and Stock Aitken Waterman production style made him one of the most recognizable voices of the Hi-NRG era.

Event: Never Gonna Give You Up goes #1 on US singles charts
Notes: This marks the anniversary of Astley's signature song reaching the top of the US Billboard Hot 100, completing its global domination after already conquering charts worldwide. The track's success was part of the British pop invasion of the late 80s and showcased the powerhouse production team of Stock Aitken Waterman. Little did anyone know that this catchy pop anthem would experience a second life in the internet age, becoming the foundation for one of the web's most enduring pranks.

Born Rick Zehringer in Union City, Indiana, this guitarist-producer celebrates another year as a rock and roll journeyman who helped shape multiple eras of American music. Derringer first gained fame as a teenager with The McCoys and their garage rock classic "Hang on Sloopy," then later became a sought-after session musician and producer working with artists like Alice Cooper and Cyndi Lauper. His guitar work and production skills bridged the gap between 60s garage rock, 70s arena rock, and 80s new wave.
The Super Freak

This marks the release of James's breakthrough album that established him as the undisputed king of funk in the early 80s. The album spawned multiple hits including the iconic "Super Freak" and "Give It to Me Baby," both of which became funk classics and later provided samples for countless hip-hop tracks. Recorded during James's creative and commercial peak, "Street Songs" perfectly captured his flamboyant persona and innovative blend of funk, rock, and new wave elements.
The first major star to sign a 360 deal with a touring company.  Deal reportedly 10 years and worth $120M.

Born William Leonard Roberts II in Clarksdale, Mississippi, this rap mogul celebrates another year as one of hip-hop's most successful entrepreneurs and larger-than-life personalities. Ross built his career on vivid drug kingpin narratives and luxurious lifestyle imagery, releasing a string of successful albums including "Teflon Don" and "God Forgives, I Don't." Beyond his solo career, he founded Maybach Music Group, which became home to artists like Meek Mill and Wale.

Born Frederick Jay Rubin, this legendary producer celebrates another year as one of the most influential figures in modern music history. Rubin co-founded Def Jam Recordings while still in college, helping launch hip-hop into the mainstream with acts like LL Cool J and the Beastie Boys, then later revitalized the careers of artists like Johnny Cash and the Red Hot Chili Peppers. His minimalist production approach and genre-crossing vision earned him recognition as one of the most important producers of all time.

Born Richard Lewis Springthorpe in Sydney, Australia, this rocker celebrates another year since becoming a teen heartthrob who successfully balanced music and acting careers. Springfield achieved massive success in the early 80s with hits like "Jessie's Girl" and "I've Done Everything for You," while simultaneously starring as Dr. Noah Drake on the soap opera General Hospital. His combination of power pop sensibilities and soap opera good looks made him one of the decade's most recognizable multimedia stars.

Born Enrique Martín Morales in San Juan, Puerto Rico, this global superstar celebrates another year since helping bring Latin pop to mainstream American audiences. Martin first gained fame as a member of Menudo before launching a solo career that exploded worldwide with "Livin' la Vida Loca" in 1999. His crossover success paved the way for other Latin artists and his energetic performances and bilingual hits made him one of the most important figures in Latin music's global expansion.

This marks the birthday of the blue-eyed soul duo's tenor half, whose soaring vocals helped define the sound of 60s pop. Hatfield partnered with Bill Medley to create some of the era's most dramatic and emotional recordings, including the Phil Spector-produced masterpiece "You've Lost That Lovin' Feelin'." Their contrasting vocal styles and powerful harmonies made them one of the most distinctive and influential acts of the decade.

Born Robyn Rihanna Fenty in Saint Michael, Barbados, this global superstar celebrates another year as one of the most successful and influential artists of the 21st century. Rihanna has sold over 250 million records worldwide with hits spanning from "Umbrella" to "Diamonds," while also building a business empire that includes Fenty Beauty and Savage X Fenty. Her ability to constantly reinvent her sound and image across pop, R&B, reggae, and dance music has made her one of the most versatile artists of her generation.

Born Richard Steven Valenzuela in Pacoima, California, this rock and roll pioneer celebrates another year despite his tragically brief career that lasted only eight months. Valens broke barriers as one of the first Mexican-American rock stars with hits like "La Bamba" and "Donna," helping to establish the Chicano presence in early rock and roll. His life was cut short at age 17 in the plane crash that also claimed Buddy Holly and J.P. Richardson, but his influence on Latino rock musicians continues to this day.

Event: A Stone, A Leaf, An Unfound Door album released
Notes: This marks the debut album release from the North Carolina-based indie folk quartet, showcasing their intricate harmonies and thoughtful songwriting. The album established River Whyless as rising stars in the Americana scene with its blend of acoustic instrumentation and lush vocal arrangements. Their sound drew comparisons to Fleet Foxes and Bon Iver while maintaining a distinctly Southern sensibility.

This self-titled EP release continued to build River Whyless's reputation in the indie folk world with refined songwriting and production. The recording showcased the band's evolution from their earlier work, featuring tighter arrangements and more confident vocal performances. The EP served as a bridge between their earlier albums and their later, more polished studio efforts.

This album release marked River Whyless's continued exploration of indie folk and Americana, building on their reputation for intricate harmonies and atmospheric arrangements. "Kindness, A Rebel" demonstrated the band's growth as songwriters and their ability to balance intimate acoustic moments with fuller band arrangements. The record reinforced their position in the contemporary folk revival movement.

Event: We All The Light album released
Notes: This album release showcased River Whyless at their most expansive, featuring lush production and their signature multi-part harmonies. "We All The Light" represented a creative peak for the North Carolina band, blending folk, indie rock, and Americana influences into a cohesive artistic statement. The record demonstrated their ability to craft both intimate acoustic songs and more ambitious, orchestrated compositions.

Born in Woodside, California, this blues-rock guitarist celebrates another year as one of the most respected players in contemporary blues and jazz fusion. Ford gained recognition through his work with L.A. Express and later with Miles Davis, before establishing himself as a formidable solo artist and session musician. His sophisticated approach to blues guitar and his ability to seamlessly blend genres has made him a favorite among both critics and fellow musicians.

Born Robert Leroy Johnson, he becomes one of the most influential blues musicians in history despite recording only 29 songs in his brief career. His legendary guitar technique and haunting vocals on tracks like "Cross Road Blues" and "Love in Vain" profoundly influenced rock legends including Eric Clapton, Keith Richards, and Led Zeppelin. The mysterious circumstances surrounding his death at age 27 and his supposed deal with the devil at a crossroads helped cement his mythical status in American music folklore.

The pioneering electronic instrument inventor revolutionized popular music with his creation of the Moog synthesizer in the 1960s. His innovations made electronic music accessible to mainstream artists, with the Moog becoming essential to the sound of everyone from The Beatles and The Doors to Kraftwerk and Giorgio Moroder. His synthesizers helped define the sonic landscape of progressive rock, disco, and eventually electronic dance music.

The British singer-songwriter became known for his smooth, soulful voice and sophisticated pop-rock sound that dominated the 1980s. His biggest hits included "Addicted to Love" and "Simply Irresistible," with their iconic music videos featuring identically-dressed female musicians becoming MTV staples.
